CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
Sat through the whole lab meeting biting my tongue while Felix presented my preprocessing idea as his own. Didn't say anything. Walked home in the rain and honestly the cold felt better than sitting in that room. At least I know the idea works — that's mine regardless of who gets credit.

Nudge: "What stopped you from speaking up?"

[ENTRY t_index=1]
People keep asking why I turned down Professor Hecht's placement like I owe them an explanation. It was a bad fit. I don't need to justify building something from scratch when the alternative is tweaking someone else's framework for two years. My project, my call.

Nudge: "You sound sure, but you haven't told your family yet."

Response: Yeah, because telling them means a two-hour phone call where my mother explains how Professor Hecht's name on my CV would open doors. She's not wrong about that part. I just can't spend two years on work that doesn't feel like mine. I'll tell them once I have something to show for it — results shut down the lecture faster than arguments do.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Three days into building my own pipeline and I'm already behind on coursework. Stayed up until four last night debugging tokenizer issues that would've been solved if I'd just used the lab's existing toolkit. My officemate Nina asked if I wanted help and I said no before I even thought about it. That was stupid. She's good at this stuff and she wasn't going to steal the project.

I keep doing this — refusing help because I need it to be entirely mine, and then drowning quietly. The fellowship application is due Friday and I haven't started the personal statement because every time I open it I think about how I'm supposed to frame myself as a team player. I don't know how to write that without it feeling like a lie, but I also don't know how to explain that I work better alone without sounding like a problem.

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
Another steering committee meeting where half the room was on their phones. I run a tight agenda and still can't get people to care about infrastructure decisions that will haunt us for years. Nobody wants to own anything hard anymore.

Nudge: "What does it cost you when they check out like that?"

Response: It makes me wonder why I bother holding the line if nobody else will.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Found out today they picked Raj for the VP role. The announcement went out at 2pm in a company-wide email. I heard about it from my skip-level before that, which I guess was supposed to soften the blow. Raj has been here six years. I have been here nineteen.

I am not saying he is unqualified. He ran the platform migration last year and it went well. But I have been running the reliability council since before he joined the company. I built the incident response process from nothing. Three directors told me privately that they assumed the role was mine. So either those conversations meant nothing or something changed that nobody told me about.

Had a one-on-one with my manager afterward. She said the decision came down to "organizational energy" and "forward-looking vision." I asked what that meant concretely and she pivoted to talking about how much they value my technical depth. That phrase again. Technical depth. It is starting to feel like a box they put you in when they do not see you leading.

Nudge: "Those three directors who assumed it was yours -- did any of them advocate for you?"

Response: I actually do not know. I assumed they did but maybe assuming was the whole problem.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Spent most of Saturday reviewing the Q3 reliability targets. Updated my tracking sheet for the team. Chen is slipping again, two missed deadlines this month.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
Cannot stop thinking about the VP thing. Told myself I was over it but then I saw Raj's new title in the org chart and my chest got tight. Thirty years and I am still a "senior individual contributor." Hate that I care this much.

Nudge: "You said you hate that you care. What would it look like if you didn't?"

Response: Honestly I have no idea. I do not know who I am if I stop keeping score.

Nudge: "You said you hate that you care. What would it look like if you didn't?"

Response: Honestly I have no idea. I do not know who I am if I stop keeping score.

[ENTRY t_index=4]
Weird thing happened at the barbecue yesterday. Tom from next door, he retired from Boeing last year, he was talking about building a deck. Not a fancy deck, just a simple one off the back of his kitchen. And he was describing the process of picking the lumber, getting the permits, measuring everything twice, and I realized I was genuinely envious. Not of the deck. Of the way he talked about it. Like it mattered to him for no reason other than he wanted to do it.

I have not built anything with my hands in years. Used to do woodworking in my twenties before the career took off. I still have my dad's old chisels in the garage somewhere, wrapped in an oilcloth. I think about them sometimes.

After the barbecue I went home and sat in the garage for a while. Found the chisels. They were right where I left them, maybe eight years ago. The handles are still good. I did not do anything with them, just held one and felt the weight. Karen came out and asked what I was doing and I said "nothing" which I guess was technically true. But it did not feel like nothing.

[ENTRY t_index=9]
First AI task force meeting was today. I spent all weekend preparing a charter document, twelve pages, frameworks, timelines, resource asks. I presented it and the CTO said "this is thorough" which I used to take as a compliment but now I wonder if it means "too much." She asked if we could "move faster and iterate." That phrase.

Raj sat in on the meeting. He did not say much but he was nodding along like he was evaluating me. Maybe I am reading into it. Probably I am reading into it. But I have been in enough rooms to know when someone is there to observe rather than participate.

Came home and Karen had dinner ready which was nice. She asked about my day and I gave her the short version. She said it sounded like a good opportunity. I agreed. We watched something on TV and I fell asleep on the couch before it ended. She put a blanket on me. I woke up at 2am still on the couch and went upstairs and she was already asleep. These are the kinds of things that keep happening and I am not sure either of us is noticing how much space there is between us now.

Nudge: "That blanket she put on you -- when did you two stop talking about the hard stuff?"

Response: We talk. Just not about the things that would require either of us to change something.

[ENTRY t_index=10]
Finished the cutting board. Walnut with maple inlay, nothing fancy but the joints are tight. Rubbed it down with mineral oil and it looks good. Brought it inside and set it on the counter.

Karen picked it up and turned it over a few times. She said "You made this?" and I said yeah. She put it down and said "It's really beautiful, Gerry." Something about the way she said my name. Have not heard her say it like that in a long time.

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
Another steering committee meeting where half the room was on their phones. I run a tight agenda and still can't get people to care about infrastructure decisions that will haunt us for years. Nobody wants to own anything hard anymore.

Nudge: "What does it cost you when they check out like that?"

Response: It makes me wonder why I bother holding the line if nobody else will.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Found out today they picked Raj for the VP role. The announcement went out at 2pm in a company-wide email. I heard about it from my skip-level before that, which I guess was supposed to soften the blow. Raj has been here six years. I have been here nineteen.

I am not saying he is unqualified. He ran the platform migration last year and it went well. But I have been running the reliability council since before he joined the company. I built the incident response process from nothing. Three directors told me privately that they assumed the role was mine. So either those conversations meant nothing or something changed that nobody told me about.

Had a one-on-one with my manager afterward. She said the decision came down to "organizational energy" and "forward-looking vision." I asked what that meant concretely and she pivoted to talking about how much they value my technical depth. That phrase again. Technical depth. It is starting to feel like a box they put you in when they do not see you leading.

Nudge: "Those three directors who assumed it was yours -- did any of them advocate for you?"

Response: I actually do not know. I assumed they did but maybe assuming was the whole problem.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Spent most of Saturday reviewing the Q3 reliability targets. Updated my tracking sheet for the team. Chen is slipping again, two missed deadlines this month.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
Cannot stop thinking about the VP thing. Told myself I was over it but then I saw Raj's new title in the org chart and my chest got tight. Thirty years and I am still a "senior individual contributor." Hate that I care this much.

Nudge: "You said you hate that you care. What would it look like if you didn't?"

Response: Honestly I have no idea. I do not know who I am if I stop keeping score.

[ENTRY t_index=4]
Weird thing happened at the barbecue yesterday. Tom from next door, he retired from Boeing last year, he was talking about building a deck. Not a fancy deck, just a simple one off the back of his kitchen. And he was describing the process of picking the lumber, getting the permits, measuring everything twice, and I realized I was genuinely envious. Not of the deck. Of the way he talked about it. Like it mattered to him for no reason other than he wanted to do it.

I have not built anything with my hands in years. Used to do woodworking in my twenties before the career took off. I still have my dad's old chisels in the garage somewhere, wrapped in an oilcloth. I think about them sometimes.

After the barbecue I went home and sat in the garage for a while. Found the chisels. They were right where I left them, maybe eight years ago. The handles are still good. I did not do anything with them, just held one and felt the weight. Karen came out and asked what I was doing and I said "nothing" which I guess was technically true. But it did not feel like nothing.

[ENTRY t_index=5]
Long week. Raj scheduled a "vision alignment" meeting for the whole engineering org and I had to sit through it smiling. He is reorganizing the reliability council under his new structure which means my committee now reports up through him. Three years of building that group and now I am presenting to someone I used to mentor.

Karen noticed I have been staying up late. She asked if I was okay and I said I was fine, just busy. She did not push it. Part of me wanted her to push it.

Nudge: "Why not just tell Karen what's actually going on?"

[ENTRY t_index=6]
Spent Sunday morning updating my LinkedIn profile. Added a few bullet points about the incident response framework and the reliability council. Looked at job postings out of curiosity. Principal engineer roles at three companies. Two of them pay more than what I make now.

Did not apply to any of them. Just screenshot the listings and saved them to a folder on my desktop called "Options." Have not decided if that is planning or procrastination.

Nudge: "That folder -- when do you think you will actually open it again?"

[ENTRY t_index=7]
Had a skip-level with the CTO today. She asked me to lead a new cross-functional task force on AI readiness. Big scope, high visibility, reports directly to her. On paper it is exactly what I have been wanting. Direct line to the top, chance to shape strategy.

But I keep thinking about why now. Two weeks after passing me over they hand me a consolation project. Raj probably suggested it. I said yes because saying no would look petty. Not sure if I actually want to do it or if I just cannot stand to be seen turning down something important.

[ENTRY t_index=8]
Took a half day and went to the hardware store. Bought some walnut stock and a new set of clamps. Nothing serious, just enough to try a small cutting board. Felt strange buying something that has nothing to do with work or credentials or org charts.

Spent the afternoon in the garage planing the wood down. My technique is rusty but the motions came back faster than I expected. I was out there for three hours and did not check my phone once. I cannot remember the last time that happened.

Nudge: "Three hours without your phone. What was going through your head out there?"

Response: Mostly just the grain direction and whether my measurements were right. That was the whole point I think. Just the thing in front of me.

[ENTRY t_index=9]
First AI task force meeting was today. I spent all weekend preparing a charter document, twelve pages, frameworks, timelines, resource asks. I presented it and the CTO said "this is thorough" which I used to take as a compliment but now I wonder if it means "too much." She asked if we could "move faster and iterate." That phrase.

Raj sat in on the meeting. He did not say much but he was nodding along like he was evaluating me. Maybe I am reading into it. Probably I am reading into it. But I have been in enough rooms to know when someone is there to observe rather than participate.

Came home and Karen had dinner ready which was nice. She asked about my day and I gave her the short version. She said it sounded like a good opportunity. I agreed. We watched something on TV and I fell asleep on the couch before it ended. She put a blanket on me. I woke up at 2am still on the couch and went upstairs and she was already asleep. These are the kinds of things that keep happening and I am not sure either of us is noticing how much space there is between us now.

Nudge: "That blanket she put on you -- when did you two stop talking about the hard stuff?"

Response: We talk. Just not about the things that would require either of us to change something.

[ENTRY t_index=10]
Finished the cutting board. Walnut with maple inlay, nothing fancy but the joints are tight. Rubbed it down with mineral oil and it looks good. Brought it inside and set it on the counter.

Karen picked it up and turned it over a few times. She said "You made this?" and I said yeah. She put it down and said "It's really beautiful, Gerry." Something about the way she said my name. Have not heard her say it like that in a long time.

[ENTRY t_index=11]
Second task force meeting went better. I cut the charter down to four pages and proposed a two-week sprint to prototype something instead of planning for another month. The CTO seemed pleased. Raj was not there this time which helped. I could just run the room without wondering if I was being graded.

Chen came to me after the meeting and said he wants to transfer onto the task force. I was surprised. He has been underperforming on my reliability team but maybe that is because he does not care about reliability work. Maybe I have been measuring him against the wrong thing. I told him I would think about it but I think I am going to say yes.

Ordered more walnut and some cherry this time. Thinking about making a small side table next. Karen asked if she could watch me work in the garage sometime and I said sure. We are going to Portland together next weekend. Her idea but I said yes without hesitating which is new. The Options folder is still on my desktop. I have not opened it in two weeks. Not sure what that means yet but it does not bother me the way it probably should.

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
The office was quiet today, most of the team out for the holiday weekend. I stayed because there was a deployment window and someone needed to be there. Rima asked why I didn't just let the junior handle it. I told her it's not about trust, it's about making sure nothing breaks while the client's systems are live.

Seventeen years at this company. The chair in my office has molded to my shape. There's something comforting about that, even if it sounds pathetic written down. I know every hallway, every server room, every shortcut in the build system. That knowledge is worth something.

Nudge: "What makes you say it sounds pathetic?"

Response: Because my cousin Fadi started his own consultancy two years ago and everyone at family dinners talks about him like he discovered electricity. Nobody asks me about my work. I think staying in one place for this long makes people assume you had no other options.

Got pulled into a meeting about "organizational restructuring." They didn't say layoffs but the room felt like a dentist's waiting area. My manager kept using phrases like "right-sizing" and "strategic realignment." I sat there nodding, taking notes, asking no questions.

Response: Whether my department is on the list. Whether seventeen years counts for anything. But you don't ask those things in front of everyone. You wait, you read the room, you figure it out without making yourself a target.

Team offsite today. The new VP of Engineering flew in from the head office in Riyadh and gave a presentation about "innovation culture" and "embracing disruption." Half the slides had stock photos of people jumping off cliffs. I wanted to ask him if he's ever actually jumped off anything, but I kept my mouth shut and clapped when everyone else clapped.

During the breakout session, one of the younger engineers, Sara, pitched an idea to rewrite our core platform in a new framework. She was passionate, articulate, the VP loved it. I could see at least fifteen ways it would break our existing integrations. But the room was electric, everyone was nodding, and I wasn't going to be the old guy who kills momentum.

After the session, my manager asked me privately what I thought. I told him the migration would take eighteen months minimum and we'd lose two major clients during the transition. He nodded and said "that's why I need you in the room, Nabil." Which is supposed to be a compliment but felt more like being told my job is to be the brakes on everyone else's engine.

I don't want to be the person who always says no. But someone has to think about what we'd lose.

Nudge: "What would it look like to say what you think without being 'the brakes'?"

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
The office was quiet today, most of the team out for the holiday weekend. I stayed because there was a deployment window and someone needed to be there. Rima asked why I didn't just let the junior handle it. I told her it's not about trust, it's about making sure nothing breaks while the client's systems are live.

Seventeen years at this company. The chair in my office has molded to my shape. There's something comforting about that, even if it sounds pathetic written down. I know every hallway, every server room, every shortcut in the build system. That knowledge is worth something.

Nudge: "What makes you say it sounds pathetic?"

Response: Because my cousin Fadi started his own consultancy two years ago and everyone at family dinners talks about him like he discovered electricity. Nobody asks me about my work. I think staying in one place for this long makes people assume you had no other options.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Got pulled into a meeting about "organizational restructuring." They didn't say layoffs but the room felt like a dentist's waiting area. My manager kept using phrases like "right-sizing" and "strategic realignment." I sat there nodding, taking notes, asking no questions.

Afterwards I walked to the parking lot and just sat in the car for twenty minutes. Checked the bank app, checked the savings, did the math again. If they let me go tomorrow, we'd be fine for six months. Maybe seven if we cut the girls' tutoring. But that's not the point. The point is I shouldn't have to be doing this math.

Nudge: "You mentioned asking no questions. What would you have asked?"

Response: Whether my department is on the list. Whether seventeen years counts for anything. But you don't ask those things in front of everyone. You wait, you read the room, you figure it out without making yourself a target.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
No news on the restructuring. Business as usual. Finished the API migration ahead of schedule. Had lunch with Marwan from the infrastructure team. He's been here twelve years and seemed calm about the whole thing. Said he's not worried because his brother-in-law works in HR. Must be nice.

Rima's mother is visiting next week. Started cleaning the guest room. The girls are complaining about having to be "presentable" all weekend. I told them it's three days, they'll survive.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
Rima's mother made a comment about how I should "think bigger" and maybe start something of my own like Fadi. I smiled and poured her more coffee. What does she want me to say? That I'm keeping her daughter's life stable while her golden nephew burns through investor money?

Nudge: "What did Rima say when her mother said that?"

Response: Nothing. She changed the subject to the girls' school. Which is its own kind of answer, I suppose. She knows I don't want to hear it, so she steers away. I'm grateful for that, but also wondering what she actually thinks.

[ENTRY t_index=4]
A recruiter reached out on LinkedIn. Startup in Dubai, fintech, "ground floor opportunity." The salary would be higher but it's a two-year contract with no guarantees after that. I looked at the job description for maybe fifteen minutes, then closed the tab.

Rima would say I should at least talk to them. My father would have said a bird in the hand. I keep thinking about what happens if the restructuring goes through and I turned down the one door that opened. But I also keep thinking about what happens if I jump and there's nothing underneath.

Nudge: "What would you need to know to feel okay about either choice?"

Response: Honestly, I don't think any amount of information would make me feel okay about it. Some decisions just feel wrong no matter which way you go.

[ENTRY t_index=5]
Thursday already. Week went by fast. Shipped the authentication module, got a thumbs up from the client. The restructuring email came out finally, my department is safe. No cuts in engineering. Marwan was right, his brother-in-law probably knew the whole time.

Funny how relief doesn't feel like happiness. It feels like unclenching. Like you've been holding your jaw tight for weeks and suddenly you notice the ache. Took the girls out for ice cream after work. Layla asked why I was being "weird and nice." Teenagers are brutal.

[ENTRY t_index=6]
Team offsite today. The new VP of Engineering flew in from the head office in Riyadh and gave a presentation about "innovation culture" and "embracing disruption." Half the slides had stock photos of people jumping off cliffs. I wanted to ask him if he's ever actually jumped off anything, but I kept my mouth shut and clapped when everyone else clapped.

During the breakout session, one of the younger engineers, Sara, pitched an idea to rewrite our core platform in a new framework. She was passionate, articulate, the VP loved it. I could see at least fifteen ways it would break our existing integrations. But the room was electric, everyone was nodding, and I wasn't going to be the old guy who kills momentum.

After the session, my manager asked me privately what I thought. I told him the migration would take eighteen months minimum and we'd lose two major clients during the transition. He nodded and said "that's why I need you in the room, Nabil." Which is supposed to be a compliment but felt more like being told my job is to be the brakes on everyone else's engine.

I don't want to be the person who always says no. But someone has to think about what we'd lose.

Nudge: "What would it look like to say what you think without being 'the brakes'?"

Thursday already. Week went by fast. Shipped the authentication module, got a thumbs up from the client. The restructuring email came out finally, my department is safe. No cuts in engineering. Marwan was right, his brother-in-law probably knew the whole time.

Funny how relief doesn't feel like happiness. It feels like unclenching. Like you've been holding your jaw tight for weeks and suddenly you notice the ache. Took the girls out for ice cream after work. Layla asked why I was being "weird and nice." Teenagers are brutal.

[ENTRY t_index=6]
Team offsite today. The new VP of Engineering flew in from the head office in Riyadh and gave a presentation about "innovation culture" and "embracing disruption." Half the slides had stock photos of people jumping off cliffs. I wanted to ask him if he's ever actually jumped off anything, but I kept my mouth shut and clapped when everyone else clapped.

During the breakout session, one of the younger engineers, Sara, pitched an idea to rewrite our core platform in a new framework. She was passionate, articulate, the VP loved it. I could see at least fifteen ways it would break our existing integrations. But the room was electric, everyone was nodding, and I wasn't going to be the old guy who kills momentum.

After the session, my manager asked me privately what I thought. I told him the migration would take eighteen months minimum and we'd lose two major clients during the transition. He nodded and said "that's why I need you in the room, Nabil." Which is supposed to be a compliment but felt more like being told my job is to be the brakes on everyone else's engine.

I don't want to be the person who always says no. But someone has to think about what we'd lose.

Nudge: "What would it look like to say what you think without being 'the brakes'?"

[ENTRY t_index=7]
Had the annual review. "Exceeds expectations" across the board. My manager wrote that I'm "the backbone of the engineering team" and "a stabilizing presence during periods of change." I should feel good about this. I do feel good about it.

Rima asked what they said and I told her. She smiled and said "of course." Like it was obvious. Like there was never any doubt. And maybe that's the thing that bothers me — not that people expect me to be reliable, but that nobody finds it surprising.

Nudge: "What would you want them to find surprising?"

Response: I don't know. Maybe that I had an idea they didn't expect. That I pushed for something risky and it worked. But that's not me, and I'm not sure I actually want it to be. I just want the option to be seen differently, even if I never use it.

[ENTRY t_index=8]
Quiet week. Wrapped up documentation for the Q3 release. Took the girls to their grandmother's on Saturday. Drove back along the coast road instead of the highway. Rima fell asleep in the passenger seat. Good drive.

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
My advisor asked today why I chose the topic of hospitality rituals in Levantine households when there are "more contemporary angles" I could take. I told her it was methodologically sound and left it at that. I don't owe anyone an explanation for why this subject matters to me. She's Swedish. She studies migration patterns. The way my grandmother set a table is not something she would understand, and I'm not interested in making it legible to her.

Came home and made musakhan from scratch, the whole process — rolling the dough, roasting the sumac onions until they were dark and sweet. Ate it alone at the kitchen table while my mother watched her show in the other room. The apartment smelled right. That was enough of an answer for one day.

Nudge: "You said you don't owe an explanation. But you came home and spent hours making your grandmother's dish. What was that about?"

Response: I like cooking. It's not that deep. The musakhan was good and I hadn't made it in a while. Sometimes dinner is just dinner.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Spent twelve hours in the library and barely got through two chapters of my lit review. My back hurts, my eyes hurt, and I keep wondering what possessed me to do this at forty-nine. Going to bed.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
My brother Faisal made a comment at Friday lunch about how I'm "still playing student" while his sons are already building careers. His eldest is twenty-six and works at a bank. I smiled and passed the hummus. What I wanted to say was that I spent twenty years running hotel operations so our family name meant something in the hospitality industry, and now I'm doing something for myself, and that should be allowed.

But it wouldn't have been the right moment. My mother was sitting right there, and she gets anxious when Faisal and I disagree. So I stayed quiet, helped clear the plates, and drove home listening to Fairuz with the windows down. The breeze off the hills felt good. I don't need Faisal's approval. I know what I'm doing and why.

Nudge: "What would have happened if you'd actually said it?"

Response: My mother would have gone quiet. Faisal would have gotten louder. His wife Rania would have changed the subject to the children, which she always does. And on the drive home I would have felt worse, not better, because I'd have disrupted the one gathering that keeps us together. The lunch matters more than being right. That's something Faisal wouldn't understand because he's never had to choose.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
Can't stop thinking about what Faisal said. Opened a bottle of that Jordanian red wine I've been saving and I'm sitting on the balcony. My mother would not approve of the wine but she went to bed early and what she doesn't see doesn't trouble her.

[ENTRY t_index=4]
Had a strange afternoon. After seminar I went to the old souk downtown instead of going home to write. I just walked. Bought a bag of roasted pistachios from the same vendor my father used to take me to when I was a boy. The man running it now is probably the vendor's grandson, same spot, same brass scale. I sat on a bench near the mosque and ate them slowly, one by one, listening to the call to prayer echo off the stone.

I haven't prayed regularly in years. My mother doesn't know this. She assumes I go to the mosque near campus on Fridays, and I don't correct her. The truth is I stopped feeling anything during prayer around the time I turned forty. The motions are still in my body — I could do them in my sleep — but the connection isn't there. And yet sitting there today, hearing the adhan bounce off those old walls while I cracked pistachios, I felt something close to what prayer used to give me. Stillness. Presence. The taste of salt and the sound of something ancient happening around me.

I don't know what to do with that. I can't tell my mother I've replaced prayer with pistachios and a bench. That would break her heart. But I also can't pretend the mosque still does for me what it used to. I'm stuck between what's honest and what's kind.

Nudge: "You described that moment on the bench as close to what prayer used to give you. What do you think changed?"

Response: I think I stopped believing that anyone was listening. Not God necessarily — I haven't worked that out — but the structure of it. The imam, the rows, the routine. It started to feel like I was performing for an audience that included my dead father and my living mother but not me. On the bench I wasn't performing for anyone. I was just a man eating pistachios in the late sun, and that was enough. Maybe what I lost wasn't faith. Maybe I lost the ability to feel things inside a frame someone else built, and I can only feel them when I stumble into them on my own. That's a lonely thought. But it felt true sitting there.

[ENTRY t_index=5]
My cohort went out for dinner last night at a rooftop restaurant in Jabal Amman. I almost didn't go. I had reading to finish and my mother had made lentil soup, and leaving her eating alone feels wrong even when she tells me to go. But I went, and I'm glad I did.

There were eight of us, all at different stages. Layla is twenty-seven and finishing her masters. Khaled is thirty-three and already published twice. And then there's me, the oldest by a decade and a half, ordering an extra plate of mezze because the lamb was extraordinary and the arak was cold and the evening air on that rooftop was exactly right. Layla asked me once why I came back to school so late, and I told her the truth — I wanted to, and I finally could, and life is too short to keep postponing the things that interest you.

She looked at me like I'd said something profound. I don't think it was. I think it was just unusual for someone my age in our context to say out loud that wanting something was reason enough to pursue it. Most of the men my brother's age would find that embarrassing. A man approaching fifty should have his affairs settled, not be starting over in a seminar room. But I'm not sure I care about what those men think anymore. The lamb was perfect. The conversation was good. I walked home through the old streets and felt awake in a way that the library doesn't give me.

Nudge: "You said you're not sure you care anymore what men your brother's age think. When did that start to shift?"

Response: I think I stopped believing that anyone was listening. Not God necessarily — I haven't worked that out — but the structure of it. The imam, the rows, the routine. [ENTRY t_index=5]
My cohort went out for dinner last night at a rooftop restaurant in Jabal Amman. I almost didn't go. I had reading to finish and my mother had made lentil soup, and leaving her eating alone feels wrong even when she tells me to go. But I went, and I'm glad I did.

There were eight of us, all at different stages. Layla is twenty-seven and finishing her masters. Khaled is thirty-three and already published twice. And then there's me, the oldest by a decade and a half, ordering an extra plate of mezze because the lamb was extraordinary and the arak was cold and the evening air on that rooftop was exactly right. Layla asked me once why I came back to school so late, and I told her the truth — I wanted to, and I finally could, and life is too short to keep postponing the things that interest you.

She looked at me like I'd said something profound. I don't think it was. I think it was just unusual for someone my age in our context to say out loud that wanting something was reason enough to pursue it. Most of the men my brother's age would find that embarrassing. A man approaching fifty should have his affairs settled, not be starting over in a seminar room. But I'm not sure I care about what those men think anymore. The lamb was perfect. The conversation was good. I walked home through the old streets and felt awake in a way that the library doesn't give me.

Nudge: "You said you're not sure you care anymore what men your brother's age think. When did that start to shift?"

[ENTRY t_index=6]
Spent the morning transcribing interviews for my fieldwork chapter. One of my subjects, a woman in her seventies from Irbid, described how she prepares mansaf for her family exactly the way her mother taught her, down to the specific way she folds the bread under the rice. She said she doesn't think about why she does it that way. She just does. Her hands know.

That's the thing I'm trying to capture in this dissertation and I keep circling around it without landing. The knowledge that lives in practice, not in explanation. My advisor wants me to theorize it and I understand why, but there's something that gets lost when you turn a grandmother's hands into a framework. I don't have a solution yet. I just know the gap is there and it bothers me.

[ENTRY t_index=7]
My mother made knafeh today. She does this maybe twice a year, always unannounced, and when I came home from campus the whole apartment smelled like orange blossom and melted cheese. She was standing at the stove watching it with that focused expression she gets, and I sat at the kitchen table pretending to read but really just watching her.

She's seventy-six. Her hands shake a little now but she still moves through the kitchen with this sureness that I don't have anywhere in my life. She poured the syrup in one smooth motion, tilted the pan, pressed the edges with a spoon. Every step inherited from her mother, who learned from her mother before that.

I ate two pieces standing at the counter before she'd even plated it properly and she swatted my hand and called me greedy. I am greedy. I wanted a third piece and I took it. The sweetness was so intense it almost hurt, that good kind of too-much where your whole mouth is alive. My mother watched me eat and looked satisfied in a way that goes deeper than food. She was feeding her son. That's a complete sentence in her world.

Later I sat with my laptop open and thought about how this is exactly what my dissertation is about, but I can't write about my own mother. It would feel like a betrayal. Like turning something sacred into data. But she's the reason I'm writing about any of this in the first place.

Nudge: "You called it a betrayal to write about your mother. What would it mean if you did?"

Response: It would mean she becomes a subject. A case study. Her knafeh becomes "an example of embodied intergenerational knowledge transfer" and the orange blossom becomes a footnote. I'd be taking something that belongs to our kitchen and putting it in a seminar room for people who have never tasted it to analyze. My advisor would call that rigorous. I'd call it taking something warm and making it cold. I know that's not a rational objection. I just feel it in my chest when I think about writing her name in an academic paper. She deserves better than being theorized.

[ENTRY t_index=8]
Canceled my library session today and drove to the Dead Sea. Told myself it was for thinking but really I just wanted to float. Paid for one of the resort day passes, the expensive one with the private beach and the buffet lunch. My fellowship stipend doesn't really cover that kind of thing but I did it anyway. Lay in the water for an hour, completely weightless, staring at the sky.

The buffet was ridiculous. Grilled prawns, tabbouleh, four kinds of baklava. I ate slowly and deliberately and didn't check my phone once. Drove home in the early evening with the salt drying on my skin and this lightness I haven't felt in weeks. I know I should have been writing. The chapter is overdue. But I can't bring myself to feel guilty about it.

Nudge: "You said you can't bring yourself to feel guilty. Is that new?"

Response: Not really. I've always been someone who needs a good meal and a change of scenery to function. The Dead Sea was beautiful and now I'm recharged. I'll write tomorrow. It's fine.

[ENTRY t_index=9]
Sat down to write after the Dead Sea trip. Got four hundred words down, all of them bad. My mother asked if I'd been to campus today and I said yes. First time I've lied to her about something this small. Too tired to think about what that means.

[ENTRY t_index=10]
Got an email from my advisor asking me to justify why my fieldwork methodology is "sufficiently distanced" from my own background. She phrased it politely but the implication is clear — studying my own culture's domestic practices introduces bias. As if the Swedish anthropologists who study Swedish folk customs don't exist. As if proximity is only a problem when you're from the region being studied.

I wrote a professional reply explaining my positionality framework and reflexive methodology. It was measured and appropriate. But sitting here now, I want to throw something. I left a career. I'm spending my savings. My mother eats dinner alone three nights a week because I'm at the library. And someone who has never set foot in a Jordanian kitchen is telling me I'm too close to the food. I need her approval to move forward with this chapter. But some nights the cost of fitting myself into their frame feels like it's eating the thing that brought me here.

Nudge: "You mentioned the cost of fitting yourself into their frame. What's the thing you're afraid of losing?"

Response: The feeling. That's what I'm afraid of losing. The reason I walked into a classroom at forty-nine wasn't the degree or the title. It was because something about the way my mother cooks, the way that woman in Irbid folds bread, the way my father used to pour coffee for guests with both hands — those things mean something, and I wanted to understand why they move me the way they do. But the more I translate that into academic language, the further I get from the feeling that started it. I'm terrified that by the time I finish this dissertation, I'll have a perfect theoretical framework for something I can no longer feel. And then what was the point? I'll have turned something alive into something correct. My advisor would say that's the job. But I don't want that to be my job. I want to hold both — the rigor and the warmth. I just don't know if the institution lets you do that.

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
My advisor asked today why I chose the topic of hospitality rituals in Levantine households when there are "more contemporary angles" I could take. I told her it was methodologically sound and left it at that. I don't owe anyone an explanation for why this subject matters to me. She's Swedish. She studies migration patterns. The way my grandmother set a table is not something she would understand, and I'm not interested in making it legible to her.

Came home and made musakhan from scratch, the whole process — rolling the dough, roasting the sumac onions until they were dark and sweet. Ate it alone at the kitchen table while my mother watched her show in the other room. The apartment smelled right. That was enough of an answer for one day.

Nudge: "You said you don't owe an explanation. But you came home and spent hours making your grandmother's dish. What was that about?"

Response: I like cooking. It's not that deep. The musakhan was good and I hadn't made it in a while. Sometimes dinner is just dinner.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Spent twelve hours in the library and barely got through two chapters of my lit review. My back hurts, my eyes hurt, and I keep wondering what possessed me to do this at forty-nine. Going to bed.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
My brother Faisal made a comment at Friday lunch about how I'm "still playing student" while his sons are already building careers. His eldest is twenty-six and works at a bank. I smiled and passed the hummus. What I wanted to say was that I spent twenty years running hotel operations so our family name meant something in the hospitality industry, and now I'm doing something for myself, and that should be allowed.

But it wouldn't have been the right moment. My mother was sitting right there, and she gets anxious when Faisal and I disagree. So I stayed quiet, helped clear the plates, and drove home listening to Fairuz with the windows down. The breeze off the hills felt good. I don't need Faisal's approval. I know what I'm doing and why.

Nudge: "What would have happened if you'd actually said it?"

Response: My mother would have gone quiet. Faisal would have gotten louder. His wife Rania would have changed the subject to the children, which she always does. And on the drive home I would have felt worse, not better, because I'd have disrupted the one gathering that keeps us together. The lunch matters more than being right. That's something Faisal wouldn't understand because he's never had to choose.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
Can't stop thinking about what Faisal said. Opened a bottle of that Jordanian red wine I've been saving and I'm sitting on the balcony. My mother would not approve of the wine but she went to bed early and what she doesn't see doesn't trouble her.

[ENTRY t_index=4]
Had a strange afternoon. After seminar I went to the old souk downtown instead of going home to write. I just walked. Bought a bag of roasted pistachios from the same vendor my father used to take me to when I was a boy. The man running it now is probably the vendor's grandson, same spot, same brass scale. I sat on a bench near the mosque and ate them slowly, one by one, listening to the call to prayer echo off the stone.

I haven't prayed regularly in years. My mother doesn't know this. She assumes I go to the mosque near campus on Fridays, and I don't correct her. The truth is I stopped feeling anything during prayer around the time I turned forty. The motions are still in my body — I could do them in my sleep — but the connection isn't there. And yet sitting there today, hearing the adhan bounce off those old walls while I cracked pistachios, I felt something close to what prayer used to give me. Stillness. Presence. The taste of salt and the sound of something ancient happening around me.

I don't know what to do with that. I can't tell my mother I've replaced prayer with pistachios and a bench. That would break her heart. But I also can't pretend the mosque still does for me what it used to. I'm stuck between what's honest and what's kind.

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
The meeting with Diego didn't go how I planned. He sat there scrolling through my routing proposal on his laptop, barely looking up, and when he finally said something it was about the formatting. The formatting. Three months of pulling delivery data, testing alternate corridors, calling warehouse contacts in Medellin -- and he wants to talk about fonts.

I should have pushed back harder. Instead I just nodded and said I'd revise it. The whole walk back to my desk I was already rewriting my argument in my head, all the things I should have said. The numbers speak for themselves. Our current route through Bucaramanga adds two days and the clients in the southern corridor keep complaining.

Part of me wonders if I backed down because he's been here fifteen years and I've been here eleven months. That shouldn't matter when the data is right there. But I sat quiet like it did matter, and now I'm stuck doing cosmetic edits on something that should already be approved.

Nudge: "Why do you think you went quiet?"

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Still at the office. Everyone else left at six but I'm going through the client satisfaction numbers from Q4 because nobody else is going to do it. Found three accounts where response times slipped below target because the team was running the old dispatch sequence I already flagged two months ago. Two months.

My mom called while I was pulling the spreadsheets. I let it go to voicemail. She'll ask why I'm still at work, then she'll ask when I'm coming home for New Year's, and I don't have answers to either one right now.

Nudge: "Are you going home for New Year's?"

Response: Probably not. There's too much to clean up here before January. She'll understand. She usually does.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Spent New Year's Eve at Lucia's apartment with a few people from the office. Nothing big -- arepas, aguardiente, the countdown on the TV. I didn't check my phone once the whole night, which felt strange at first but I stopped noticing after a while. Lucia made fun of me for not knowing any of the songs on the playlist.

On January first I went for a run along the ciclovia and came back with an idea for restructuring the southern corridor drops. Not the full routing plan Diego shut down -- something smaller, a pilot with just the Cali deliveries. If I can show a cost reduction on one leg, the rest of the argument builds itself. Nobody has to approve a pilot. I can just run it with the three drivers who already told me the current route doesn't make sense.

I drafted the pilot scope on my laptop sitting on Lucia's couch while everyone was watching a movie. Felt good to have something concrete again instead of waiting for permission. I want to own how this unfolds, start to finish.

My mom called while I was pulling the spreadsheets. I let it go to voicemail. She'll ask why I'm still at work, then she'll ask when I'm coming home for New Year's, and I don't have answers to either one right now.

Nudge: "Are you going home for New Year's?"

Response: Probably not. There's too much to clean up here before January. She'll understand. She usually does.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Spent New Year's Eve at Lucia's apartment with a few people from the office. Nothing big -- arepas, aguardiente, the countdown on the TV. I didn't check my phone once the whole night, which felt strange at first but I stopped noticing after a while. Lucia made fun of me for not knowing any of the songs on the playlist.

On January first I went for a run along the ciclovia and came back with an idea for restructuring the southern corridor drops. Not the full routing plan Diego shut down -- something smaller, a pilot with just the Cali deliveries. If I can show a cost reduction on one leg, the rest of the argument builds itself. Nobody has to approve a pilot. I can just run it with the three drivers who already told me the current route doesn't make sense.

I drafted the pilot scope on my laptop sitting on Lucia's couch while everyone was watching a movie. Felt good to have something concrete again instead of waiting for permission. I want to own how this unfolds, start to finish.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
The Cali pilot is running. Three drivers, rerouted through Palmira instead of the standard loop. I didn't tell Diego. I told the drivers it was a test week and tracked the first four deliveries myself from the dashboard. Average time dropped by six hours per run. Six hours. That's not a font issue.

I built a one-page summary with the numbers and sent it to the full operations chat, not just Diego. Maybe that was aggressive. Lucia said I should have gone through him first, and she's probably right in terms of politics. But if I go through him it sits in his inbox for two weeks and nothing changes. I've watched that happen before.

He hasn't responded yet. Part of me is nervous but mostly I feel like I did the thing I actually wanted to do instead of waiting around editing slide decks. If the numbers hold through Friday, the argument is over. I want this to stand on the work, not on who approved it.

[ENTRY t_index=4]
Diego pulled me into his office yesterday and I thought he was going to tear into me about the pilot but he just asked for the full data set. Response: I don't know. Something that makes the last three months feel like they counted.

[ENTRY t_index=5]
Call with the VP happened. Forty-five minutes. She asked detailed questions about methodology, cost projections, scalability to other corridors. She knew what she was talking about, which made it feel real -- not like presenting to someone who just wants to check a box.

At the end she said she wants me to present to the regional leadership group in February. A full presentation, not a one-pager. My stomach dropped when she said it, not because I'm scared but because I've never had that kind of stage before and I want it to go perfectly. I already know I'm going to over-prepare.

Called my mom after to tell her. She said "mija, eat something, you sound wired." She's not wrong. I had an empanada for lunch and nothing since. But this is the thing I've been pushing for since I got here and it's actually happening. Not because someone handed it to me -- because I built it and put it out there myself.

Nudge: "What does a perfect presentation look like to you?"

Response: Numbers that nobody can argue with, questions I already have answers for, and walking out knowing they couldn't have gotten that analysis from anyone else on the team.

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
The meeting with Diego didn't go how I planned. He sat there scrolling through my routing proposal on his laptop, barely looking up, and when he finally said something it was about the formatting. The formatting. Three months of pulling delivery data, testing alternate corridors, calling warehouse contacts in Medellin -- and he wants to talk about fonts.

I should have pushed back harder. Instead I just nodded and said I'd revise it. The whole walk back to my desk I was already rewriting my argument in my head, all the things I should have said. The numbers speak for themselves. Our current route through Bucaramanga adds two days and the clients in the southern corridor keep complaining.

Part of me wonders if I backed down because he's been here fifteen years and I've been here eleven months. That shouldn't matter when the data is right there. But I sat quiet like it did matter, and now I'm stuck doing cosmetic edits on something that should already be approved.

Nudge: "Why do you think you went quiet?"

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Still at the office. Everyone else left at six but I'm going through the client satisfaction numbers from Q4 because nobody else is going to do it. Found three accounts where response times slipped below target because the team was running the old dispatch sequence I already flagged two months ago. Two months.

My mom called while I was pulling the spreadsheets. I let it go to voicemail. She'll ask why I'm still at work, then she'll ask when I'm coming home for New Year's, and I don't have answers to either one right now.

Nudge: "Are you going home for New Year's?"

Response: Probably not. There's too much to clean up here before January. She'll understand. She usually does.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Spent New Year's Eve at Lucia's apartment with a few people from the office. Nothing big -- arepas, aguardiente, the countdown on the TV. I didn't check my phone once the whole night, which felt strange at first but I stopped noticing after a while. Lucia made fun of me for not knowing any of the songs on the playlist.

On January first I went for a run along the ciclovia and came back with an idea for restructuring the southern corridor drops. Not the full routing plan Diego shut down -- something smaller, a pilot with just the Cali deliveries. If I can show a cost reduction on one leg, the rest of the argument builds itself. Nobody has to approve a pilot. I can just run it with the three drivers who already told me the current route doesn't make sense.

I drafted the pilot scope on my laptop sitting on Lucia's couch while everyone was watching a movie. Felt good to have something concrete again instead of waiting for permission. I want to own how this unfolds, start to finish.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
The Cali pilot is running. Three drivers, rerouted through Palmira instead of the standard loop. I didn't tell Diego. I told the drivers it was a test week and tracked the first four deliveries myself from the dashboard. Average time dropped by six hours per run. Six hours. That's not a font issue.

I built a one-page summary with the numbers and sent it to the full operations chat, not just Diego. Maybe that was aggressive. Lucia said I should have gone through him first, and she's probably right in terms of politics. But if I go through him it sits in his inbox for two weeks and nothing changes. I've watched that happen before.

He hasn't responded yet. Part of me is nervous but mostly I feel like I did the thing I actually wanted to do instead of waiting around editing slide decks. If the numbers hold through Friday, the argument is over. I want this to stand on the work, not on who approved it.

[ENTRY t_index=9]

I don't care.

I didn't check my phone once the whole night, which felt strange at first but I stopped noticing after a while. Lucia made fun of me for not knowing any of the songs on the playlist.

On January first I went for a run along the ciclovia and came back with an idea for restructuring the southern corridor drops. Not the full routing plan Diego shut down -- something smaller, a pilot with just the Cali deliveries. If I can show a cost reduction on one leg, the rest of the argument builds itself. Nobody has to approve a pilot. I can just run it with the three drivers who already told me the current route doesn't make sense.

I drafted the pilot scope on my laptop sitting on Lucia's couch while everyone was watching a movie. Felt good to have something concrete again instead of waiting for permission. I want to own how this unfolds, start to finish.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
The Cali pilot is running. Three drivers, rerouted through Palmira instead of the standard loop. I didn't tell Diego. I told the drivers it was a test week and tracked the first four deliveries myself from the dashboard. Average time dropped by six hours per run. Six hours. That's not a font issue.

I built a one-page summary with the numbers and sent it to the full operations chat, not just Diego. Maybe that was aggressive. Lucia said I should have gone through him first, and she's probably right in terms of politics. But if I go through him it sits in his inbox for two weeks and nothing changes. I've watched that happen before.

He hasn't responded yet. Part of me is nervous but mostly I feel like I did the thing I actually wanted to do instead of waiting around editing slide decks. If the numbers hold through Friday, the argument is over. I want this to stand on the work, not on who approved it.

[ENTRY t_index=4]
Diego pulled me into his office yesterday and I thought he was going to tear into me about the pilot but he just asked for the full data set. No commentary, no lecture, just "send me the spreadsheet." I sent it within the hour. He forwarded it to the VP of ops in Mexico City. I know because the VP emailed me directly asking for a call next week.

That call could be anything. Could be a reprimand, could be an expansion. Either way the work is out there now and it's mine. I keep refreshing my email like that's going to make the calendar invite appear faster. Need to stop doing that.

Nudge: "What are you hoping she says?"

Response: I don't know. Something that makes the last three months feel like they counted.

[ENTRY t_index=5]
Call with the VP happened. Forty-five minutes. She asked detailed questions about methodology, cost projections, scalability to other corridors. She knew what she was talking about, which made it feel real -- not like presenting to someone who just wants to check a box.

At the end she said she wants me to present to the regional leadership group in February. A full presentation, not a one-pager. My stomach dropped when she said it, not because I'm scared but because I've never had that kind of stage before and I want it to go perfectly. I already know I'm going to over-prepare.

Called my mom after to tell her. She said "mija, eat something, you sound wired." She's not wrong. I had an empanada for lunch and nothing since. But this is the thing I've been pushing for since I got here and it's actually happening. Not because someone handed it to me -- because I built it and put it out there myself.

Nudge: "What does a perfect presentation look like to you?"

Response: Numbers that nobody can argue with, questions I already have answers for, and walking out knowing they couldn't have gotten that analysis from anyone else on the team.

[ENTRY t_index=6]
Stayed up until three working on the deck. Caught myself copying Diego's formatting from an old presentation because I thought it would make the leadership group take it more seriously. Closed the file and went to bed feeling off about it.

[ENTRY t_index=7]
Lucia told me today that some people on the team think I'm "trying to skip levels." She said it like she was doing me a favor by telling me, and maybe she was, but it stung. I've been busting myself on this project and the fact that doing good work gets read as political maneuvering makes me want to throw my laptop into the Magdalena.

I asked her who said it and she wouldn't tell me. Fine. But now I'm sitting here wondering if I should tone it down for the February presentation or just go harder. Toning it down feels like giving up ground I earned. Going harder might prove their point. I don't know.

[ENTRY t_index=8]
Finished the presentation draft this morning. Fourteen slides, every number sourced, three scenario projections. I ran through it out loud twice in my apartment and timed it at eighteen minutes, which leaves room for questions. My neighbor probably thinks I'm losing it.

Went to my abuela's for almuerzo after. She made sancocho and didn't ask about work at all, just told me about her friend Doris who's fighting with her landlord. Sat there eating soup and listening and realized I hadn't thought about the presentation for almost two hours. That's probably the longest I've gone without thinking about it in weeks.

Nudge: "How did the two hours feel?"

Response: Good. Like my brain got to stop running for a bit. I forgot what that was like.

[ENTRY t_index=9]
Diego sent me "feedback" on the presentation draft. Half of it is adding his name to the second slide as a co-contributor. He didn't contribute. He sat on my original proposal for weeks and only engaged after I went around him. Now he wants credit on the version that works.

I know the smart move is probably to add his name and keep the peace. Everyone keeps telling me to "play the game." But I didn't build this by playing the game. I built it by ignoring the game and doing the actual work. If I put his name on it, what am I even presenting? Someone else's stamp on my project. I'm going to push back. Maybe that's stupid. I don't care.

Nudge: "Do you actually not care?"

[ENTRY t_index=10]
Presentation went well. The VP asked me to lead a corridor optimization working group across three countries. Diego shook my hand after and didn't mention the co-credit thing. I slept eleven hours last night and I'm still tired, but it's the kind of tired where you know you did the thing.

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
My brother-in-law made a comment at Sunday dinner about how I must enjoy "having so much free time now" and I've been thinking about it all week. Free time? I coordinate three different after-school activities, manage the household budget down to the krone, and I've been teaching myself upholstery techniques that would take most people years to master. Just because there's no office title attached doesn't mean I'm sitting around watching television.

I know I shouldn't let it get to me. But there's this part of me that keeps wanting to list all the things I've done this month, all the skills I've picked up, all the ways I've improved how our home runs. The furniture business actually got two new orders last week. That's something, isn't it? Sometimes I feel like I'm running faster than I ever did at the firm, just with nobody keeping score anymore.

Nudge: "It sounds like that comment really stayed with you. What does "keeping score" mean to you now, outside of the traditional work context?"

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Finished the oak sideboard restoration today. Client picked it up and actually teared up a bit when she saw it. Said it had been her grandmother's and she'd been looking for someone to bring it back properly for years. Took about forty hours total across two weeks.

Started sketching plans for a home workshop expansion. The garage layout isn't working anymore, especially with winter keeping me indoors more. Found some modular storage systems from a German supplier that could work. The twins helped me measure the space after school. Emma is surprisingly good at holding a tape measure steady. I'm thinking if I reorganize everything by March, I could take on bigger pieces. Maybe even start documenting the process to teach others eventually.

Nudge: "That's a meaningful project completion. What was most satisfying about the sideboard work - the technical challenge, the client's response, or something else?"

Response: Honestly? The technical part. Matching the original veneer grain pattern took me three tries and about five hours of research into 1920s Danish furniture styles. When I finally got it right, I took photos before and after just for my own records. The client's reaction was nice, genuinely touching even, but I knew it was good work before she saw it. The satisfaction came earlier, when I stepped back and could see I'd actually pulled it off. The workshop expansion isn't just about space. I want to be able to take on restoration challenges I can't even attempt right now with the current setup.

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
My mother called to remind me about the novena for Tía Carmen's anniversary next week. Didn't need the reminder — it's been circled on my calendar since January. When I was small I used to think these gatherings were just adults talking over coffee, but now I understand them differently. Nine nights of prayer, nine nights of family filling up the house. There's something steady about it.

I taught my third graders how to fold paper flowers today for the altar at school. One little boy asked why we do it every year and I told him the same thing my grandmother told me: because the dead don't really leave if we keep making space for them. He seemed satisfied with that. Sometimes children accept what adults forget — that repetition isn't emptiness, it's a choice to keep something alive.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Carlos asked to meet my family this weekend. I made up something about it being a busy week with the novena. He looked hurt. I feel like I'm lying to two worlds at once — telling him we just need more time, telling my mother I'm too focused on work to date. Neither story is true. The true story is that I don't know how to bring a divorced man into a family that still whispers about my cousin's civil ceremony.

Nudge: "What would happen if you just told your mother the truth?"

Response: She wouldn't yell. That's not how she operates. She'd get quiet and then start asking gentle questions that feel like tiny knives — How long has this been going on? Has he been to confession? What does his family think about all this? And I'd have no good answers. I don't even know if I want to marry him. I just want space to figure that out without the weight of everyone's expectations pressing down on me before I've made up my own mind.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
The new principal at school announced we're "modernizing" the curriculum. Tablets for third graders. Interactive lessons instead of textbooks. She presented it like everyone should be excited but I sat in that meeting thinking about Sister Magdalena who taught at this school for forty years and how her chalkboard handwriting was so beautiful my mother still talks about it. I'm not against technology — I use my phone constantly, I'm not a dinosaur — but something about the way the principal said "we need to move past old methods" made me want to dig my heels in.

After the meeting Roberto from fifth grade asked what I thought and I said it sounded fine. I don't know why I didn't say what I was really thinking. Maybe because I didn't want to be the young teacher defending old ways. Maybe because I don't even know if I'm right. The kids might love the tablets. Sister Magdalena isn't here to ask.

I keep thinking about how my mother taught me to read with a book of saints' stories, turning actual pages, her finger tracing the words. What gets lost when everything becomes a screen? Or am I just afraid of change and calling it something nicer?

Nudge: "You said you didn't speak up. What would you have said if you had?"

Response: That maybe we should pilot it in one classroom first instead of rolling it out everywhere at once. That the older teachers who've been here for decades deserve to have their methods respected, not treated like problems to be solved. That learning isn't just information transfer — it's also ritual, routine, the physical feeling of a pencil in your hand. But standing up in a staff meeting to say "I think ritual matters" sounds ridiculous. So I stayed quiet and now I'm typing it into my phone at 11pm instead.

[ENTRY t_index=4]
My friend Valentina says I'm "too attached to the past." She means it gently but it still stings. She left Guadalajara for Monterrey and barely comes back for holidays now. Good for her. I'm not running from anything by staying. This is where I'm needed. My grandmother is eighty-three. My students know me. The roots here are mine.

Nudge: "What about her comment bothered you most?"

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
My mother called to remind me about the novena for Tía Carmen's anniversary next week. Didn't need the reminder — it's been circled on my calendar since January. When I was small I used to think these gatherings were just adults talking over coffee, but now I understand them differently. Nine nights of prayer, nine nights of family filling up the house. There's something steady about it.

I taught my third graders how to fold paper flowers today for the altar at school. One little boy asked why we do it every year and I told him the same thing my grandmother told me: because the dead don't really leave if we keep making space for them. He seemed satisfied with that. Sometimes children accept what adults forget — that repetition isn't emptiness, it's a choice to keep something alive.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Carlos asked to meet my family this weekend. I made up something about it being a busy week with the novena. He looked hurt. I feel like I'm lying to two worlds at once — telling him we just need more time, telling my mother I'm too focused on work to date. Neither story is true. The true story is that I don't know how to bring a divorced man into a family that still whispers about my cousin's civil ceremony.

Nudge: "What would happen if you just told your mother the truth?"

Response: She wouldn't yell. That's not how she operates. She'd get quiet and then start asking gentle questions that feel like tiny knives — How long has this been going on? Has he been to confession? What does his family think about all this? And I'd have no good answers. I don't even know if I want to marry him. I just want space to figure that out without the weight of everyone's expectations pressing down on me before I've made up my own mind.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Spent the evening at Abuela's preparing food for tomorrow's final night of the novena. Chopped onions until my eyes burned. My cousin Daniela brought her new baby and everyone passed him around the kitchen like a small warm blessing. The prayers start at seven. I'll be there early to set up chairs like I have been every year since I was fourteen.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
The new principal at school announced we're "modernizing" the curriculum. Tablets for third graders. Interactive lessons instead of textbooks. She presented it like everyone should be excited but I sat in that meeting thinking about Sister Magdalena who taught at this school for forty years and how her chalkboard handwriting was so beautiful my mother still talks about it. I'm not against technology — I use my phone constantly, I'm not a dinosaur — but something about the way the principal said "we need to move past old methods" made me want to dig my heels in.

After the meeting Roberto from fifth grade asked what I thought and I said it sounded fine. I don't know why I didn't say what I was really thinking. Maybe because I didn't want to be the young teacher defending old ways. Maybe because I don't even know if I'm right. The kids might love the tablets. Sister Magdalena isn't here to ask.

I keep thinking about how my mother taught me to read with a book of saints' stories, turning actual pages, her finger tracing the words. What gets lost when everything becomes a screen? Or am I just afraid of change and calling it something nicer?

Nudge: "You said you didn't speak up. What would you have said if you had?"

Response: That maybe we should pilot it in one classroom first instead of rolling it out everywhere at once. That the older teachers who've been here for decades deserve to have their methods respected, not treated like problems to be solved. That learning isn't just information transfer — it's also ritual, routine, the physical feeling of a pencil in your hand. But standing up in a staff meeting to say "I think ritual matters" sounds ridiculous. So I stayed quiet and now I'm typing it into my phone at 11pm instead.

[ENTRY t_index=4]
My friend Valentina says I'm "too attached to the past." She means it gently but it still stings. She left Guadalajara for Monterrey and barely comes back for holidays now. Good for her. I'm not running from anything by staying. This is where I'm needed. My grandmother is eighty-three. My students know me. The roots here are mine.

Nudge: "What about her comment bothered you most?"

[ENTRY t_index=5]
Carlos and I had a real fight. Not yelling — we don't do that — but the cold kind where you both know something broke. He asked if I was ashamed of him and I didn't answer fast enough. I'm not ashamed. I'm scared. There's a difference but I couldn't find the words to explain it while he was standing there looking at me like he'd already made up his mind about what my silence meant.

My parents have been married for thirty-two years. They met at church, got engaged with my grandfather's blessing, had the wedding everyone still talks about. That's the script. I don't know how to write a different one and still feel like myself. Carlos doesn't fit the script but neither does walking away from him. I lay awake until three trying to figure out which betrayal I could live with.

Nudge: "Is there a version of this where nobody gets betrayed?"

Response: I want to say yes. The optimistic answer is that my family loves me and they'll accept whoever makes me happy. My cousin got pushback about her civil ceremony and now everyone's fine at Christmas dinner. But I know my mother. She carries things quietly for years. She'd show up to the wedding and smile and then I'd catch her crying in the kitchen when she thinks no one's looking. I don't know if I can build a life on top of that image. Maybe that's my problem — I'm not choosing between Carlos and my family. I'm choosing between the version of me who disappoints people and the version who disappears herself to keep everyone comfortable.

[ENTRY t_index=6]
Went to confession today for the first time in months. Didn't plan to — I was walking past the church after buying bread and the door was open. Father Miguel has known me since my first communion so there was no hiding behind anonymity. I told him I've been lying to my mother about something important. He didn't ask what. Just waited.

I ended up talking for twenty minutes about Carlos, about feeling stuck, about how the rules I grew up with don't seem to have instructions for this situation. He said something I keep turning over: "The tradition doesn't ask you to stop living. It asks you to bring your life to it honestly." I don't know exactly what that means yet but it felt like permission to stop pretending there's a perfect answer.

After confession I sat in the pew where my grandmother always sits and just breathed. The church smelled the same way it did when I was seven. Some things don't change and today that felt like comfort instead of a cage.

[ENTRY t_index=7]
School starts back next week. Spent the afternoon organizing my classroom. Put up the same San Francisco de Asis poster my mother gave me my first year teaching. The corners are getting worn. Considered replacing it but decided against it. Some things are allowed to show their age.

[ENTRY t_index=8]
I told my mother about Carlos. Not everything — not the divorce — but that I've been seeing someone. She asked when she could meet him and I panicked and said "soon" which is what I always say. She knows me too well. She could hear the hesitation and asked what I wasn't telling her.

I almost said it. The words were right there. But then my abuela came into the room asking about dinner and the moment passed and I felt relief and shame in equal measure. Relief because I got more time. Shame because I'm a grown woman hiding in the kitchen of my childhood home, still arranging my life around moments that are safe.

Carlos deserves better than being my secret. I deserve better than feeling like a coward. Something has to break.

Nudge: "What would breaking look like for you?"

Response: Telling the full truth and accepting whatever comes after. Not performing okay-ness at the next family gathering. Letting my mother feel her feelings without me trying to manage them. Maybe it looks like a difficult Sunday dinner where Carlos sits across from my father and answers questions about his first marriage. Maybe it looks like my grandmother going quiet in that way she does when she disapproves. I can survive that. I've survived other hard things. What I can't survive is another six months of living in two separate worlds that never touch.

[ENTRY t_index=9]
Brought Carlos to Sunday lunch at my parents' house. My hands were shaking when we walked through the door. My mother had set an extra place — she knew I wouldn't come alone after our last conversation. She's always known more than she lets on.

It wasn't easy. There were silences that stretched too long and my father asked pointed questions about where Carlos sees himself in five years. But my grandmother held his hand when he talked about his daughter from his first marriage, and said children are always a blessing no matter the circumstances. That's not the same as approval but it's not rejection either.

After dessert, while Carlos helped my father clear plates, my mother pulled me aside and said "I don't understand all of it but I can see he's good to you." That's enough for now. Maybe that's what Father Miguel meant — bringing my life to the tradition honestly doesn't mean it fits perfectly. It just means I stop hiding the pieces that don't.
