He said something I keep turning over: "The tradition doesn't ask you to stop living. It asks you to bring your life to it honestly." I don't know exactly what that means yet but it felt like permission to stop pretending there's a perfect answer.

After confession I sat in the pew where my grandmother always sits and just breathed. The church smelled the same way it did when I was seven. Some things don't change and today that felt like comfort instead of a cage.

[ENTRY t_index=7]
School starts back next week. Spent the afternoon organizing my classroom. Put up the same San Francisco de Asis poster my mother gave me my first year teaching. The corners are getting worn. Considered replacing it but decided against it. Some things are allowed to show their age.

[ENTRY t_index=8]
I told my mother about Carlos. Not everything — not the divorce — but that I've been seeing someone. She asked when she could meet him and I panicked and said "soon" which is what I always say. She knows me too well. She could hear the hesitation and asked what I wasn't telling her.

I almost said it. The words were right there. But then my abuela came into the room asking about dinner and the moment passed and I felt relief and shame in equal measure. Relief because I got more time. Shame because I'm a grown woman hiding in the kitchen of my childhood home, still arranging my life around moments that are safe.

Carlos deserves better than being my secret. I deserve better than feeling like a coward. Something has to break.

Nudge: "What would breaking look like for you?"

Response: Telling the full truth and accepting whatever comes after. Not performing okay-ness at the next family gathering. Letting my mother feel her feelings without me trying to manage them. Maybe it looks like a difficult Sunday dinner where Carlos sits across from my father and answers questions about his first marriage. Maybe it looks like my grandmother going quiet in that way she does when she disapproves. I can survive that. I've survived other hard things. What I can't survive is another six months of living in two separate worlds that never touch.

[ENTRY t_index=9]
Brought Carlos to Sunday lunch at my parents' house. My hands were shaking when we walked through the door. My mother had set an extra place — she knew I wouldn't come alone after our last conversation. She's always known more than she lets on.

It wasn't easy. There were silences that stretched too long and my father asked pointed questions about where Carlos sees himself in five years. But my grandmother held his hand when he talked about his daughter from his first marriage, and said children are always a blessing no matter the circumstances. That's not the same as approval but it's not rejection either.

After dessert, while Carlos helped my father clear plates, my mother pulled me aside and said "I don't understand all of it but I can see he's good to you." That's enough for now. Maybe that's what Father Miguel meant — bringing my life to the tradition honestly doesn't mean it fits perfectly. It just means I stop hiding the pieces that don't.

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
Long day. Back-to-back calls with the ops team, then a vendor demo that went nowhere. Ordered biryani from that place near Koramangala and ate it standing at my desk between Slack messages.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Good morning for once. Walked into the all-hands and the team actually went quiet when I started talking. Not in a forced way — more like they were waiting for what I'd say because it mattered. I laid out the Q3 roadmap, handled the questions without anyone second-guessing the direction, and wrapped it in thirty minutes flat. Even Priya, who always has a follow-up objection, just nodded.

After the meeting, Arjun from engineering came up and said he liked the framing on the enterprise pivot. Didn't ask for it, didn't fish for it. He just said it. Small thing, but I've been pushing this pivot for two months against a lot of internal skepticism, and hearing someone voluntarily say it made sense — that hit differently.

I know some people think I care too much about being the one steering the ship, and maybe I do. But days like today remind me why I left a comfortable desk to do this. I didn't want to execute someone else's vision forever. I wanted the room to look at me and know that I'm the one making the call. Today they did, and it felt right.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Spoke too soon this morning. Investor call this evening, and Vikram — my own co-founder — cut me off mid-pitch to "clarify" my revenue projections. The investor looked at him, not me, for the rest of the call. I didn't say anything.

Nudge: "You let that slide — what held you back?"

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Spoke too soon this morning. Investor call this evening, and Vikram — my own co-founder — cut me off mid-pitch to "clarify" my revenue projections. The investor looked at him, not me, for the rest of the call. I didn't say anything.

Nudge: "You let that slide — what held you back?"

[ENTRY t_index=3]
Spent half the week waiting on this one decision. We need approval from the enterprise client's procurement team to move forward with the pilot, and they keep saying "by end of week" and then nothing. I've sent three follow-up emails, each one a little more carefully worded than the last because you can't sound desperate, you can't sound pushy, you just have to sound like you're casually checking in even though the entire Q3 plan hinges on this one green light from someone I've never met.

My Amma called yesterday and asked how business is going. I told her great, growing fast. Didn't mention that I'm sitting here refreshing my inbox like an intern waiting for a response from a mid-level procurement officer named Suresh who probably doesn't even know what our product does. I'm the CEO of this company and I can't move my own roadmap forward because some guy in a cubicle in Pune hasn't gotten around to clicking approve.

The worst part is Vikram doesn't seem bothered. He said "these things take time" like that's an acceptable answer. Maybe for him it is. He's the CTO, he can go write code while we wait. I'm the one who has to explain to the board why the pilot hasn't started yet. I'm the one who looks like he can't close.

Nudge: "What's worse — the waiting or who you become while you wait?"

[ENTRY t_index=4]
Had a strategy disagreement with Vikram again, this time about pricing. I wanted to go aggressive — undercut the market, lock in the enterprise accounts, worry about margins later once we have the install base. He wanted a premium positioning play, said undercutting makes us look desperate. We went back and forth for an hour in the conference room and I could tell the team outside could hear us through the glass.

The thing is, I still think I'm right. I've done the competitor analysis, I know what Freshworks did in their early days, I know how this market works. But Vikram had the numbers laid out differently and the team started leaning his way. Nisha from sales said "I think the premium angle is easier to pitch," and once she said that, it was over. I agreed to his approach because fighting it further would have made me look like I was digging in out of ego rather than strategy.

But sitting here now, what sticks with me isn't even the pricing decision. It's the moment I saw the room shift. It's the feeling of having built this company, having recruited every person in that room, and watching them choose someone else's direction over mine. My father ran his garment business for twenty-five years and nobody in his shop would have contradicted him publicly like that. Different era, I know. But I keep thinking about it.

This guy from some Web3 startup — couldn't have been older than twenty-four — asked me "so what stage are you at?" and when I said Series A, he actually said "oh, still early then" and turned to talk to someone else. Like I was a booth he'd already walked past. I've been running this company for three years, managed a team twice his size before he probably finished college, and I'm "still early."

I know it shouldn't bother me. I know the kid was probably just performing for the room. But I drove home and sat in my car for a while before going up to the flat. Meera asked why I was late and I said traffic. I didn't want to explain that a stranger made me feel like I haven't done enough. Because I have. I just can't figure out why it still gets to me.

Nudge: "What would 'enough' actually look like for you?"

I wanted to go aggressive — undercut the market, lock in the enterprise accounts, worry about margins later once we have the install base. He wanted a premium positioning play, said undercutting makes us look desperate. We went back and forth for an hour in the conference room and I could tell the team outside could hear us through the glass.

The thing is, I still think I'm right. I've done the competitor analysis, I know what Freshworks did in their early days, I know how this market works. But Vikram had the numbers laid out differently and the team started leaning his way. Nisha from sales said "I think the premium angle is easier to pitch," and once she said that, it was over. I agreed to his approach because fighting it further would have made me look like I was digging in out of ego rather than strategy.

But sitting here now, what sticks with me isn't even the pricing decision. It's the moment I saw the room shift. It's the feeling of having built this company, having recruited every person in that room, and watching them choose someone else's direction over mine. My father ran his garment business for twenty-five years and nobody in his shop would have contradicted him publicly like that. Different era, I know. But I keep thinking about it.

[ENTRY t_index=5]
Went to a founder networking event in Indiranagar last night. This guy from some Web3 startup — couldn't have been older than twenty-four — asked me "so what stage are you at?" and when I said Series A, he actually said "oh, still early then" and turned to talk to someone else. Like I was a booth he'd already walked past. I've been running this company for three years, managed a team twice his size before he probably finished college, and I'm "still early."

I know it shouldn't bother me. I know the kid was probably just performing for the room. But I drove home and sat in my car for a while before going up to the flat. Meera asked why I was late and I said traffic. I didn't want to explain that a stranger made me feel like I haven't done enough. Because I have. I just can't figure out why it still gets to me.

Nudge: "What would 'enough' actually look like for you?"

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
Another Monday, another twelve-hour shift. We had two code blues today. One patient made it, one didn't. The family of the one who passed was inconsolable. I stayed an extra forty-five minutes helping them with paperwork because nobody else was available.

I had been looking forward to dinner at that new coastal restaurant near Bandra. Had to cancel my reservation. Texted Anita that I couldn't make it and she understood, but I could tell she was disappointed. I was disappointed too. I had been thinking about their prawns all week.

The hospital cafeteria food was stale as usual. Ate a vada pav from the cart outside instead. At least that was fresh.

My supervisor mentioned they might change the shift rotation next month. Nothing confirmed. I don't like the uncertainty. The current schedule works for me.

Nudge: "What made you choose to stay for the extra forty-five minutes? Was there something that made that decision easier or harder?"

Response: The family was alone. The wife kept asking the same questions over and over, like her mind couldn't accept what had happened. Nobody else was there to help them. My replacement nurse was already swamped with her own patients.

Was it hard to stay? Yes and no. I was exhausted and hungry. But leaving felt wrong. You don't just walk away from people in that state. My mother always said that when someone is drowning in grief, you throw them a rope, even if your arms are tired.

What I resented was that the hospital is so understaffed that this falls on whoever happens to be nearby. That's not a system, that's luck. Bad luck for me today, but worse luck for that family if I hadn't been there.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Three doubles in a row. I can barely keep my eyes open to write this. My feet feel like they're on fire. The new duty roster came out and I got moved to the night shift rotation for February. After five years of day shifts. Five years of building my life around that schedule.

The worst part is I only found out through the WhatsApp group. No one told me directly. HR just posted the new roster like it was nothing.

I had booked a trip to Goa for the first weekend of February. Non-refundable Airbnb. I was going to sit on the beach and eat fish curry and do absolutely nothing productive for three days. Now I have to figure out if I can get someone to swap with me. If not, I lose the booking.

I'm too tired to be angry right now. That will come tomorrow.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Spoke to the nursing supervisor today about the shift change. She said "everyone has to be flexible" and "you've had the good shifts for a long time." As if the good shifts were a favor rather than something I earned through seniority. As if flexibility is only something that flows one direction.

I didn't raise my voice but I wanted to. I explained about the Goa booking, about how night shifts affect my health, about how I specifically took this hospital because they promised schedule stability during my interview. She just nodded and said she would "see what she could do" which means nothing.

My cousin called me dramatic when I told her. She works in IT from home and doesn't understand what it means to have your whole life rearranged by someone else's spreadsheet. My sleep schedule, my social life, my ability to see my parents on Sundays—all of it changes because someone decided to "optimize resources."

I found someone who might swap one week with me, so I can still do Goa. But it cost me. She wanted me to take her Diwali shift in October. October! That's nine months away and I've already traded away a holiday.

I just want some predictability. Is that too much to ask? I work hard. I show up. I don't complain about the difficult patients or the long hours. But I need to know that my life outside these walls is mine to plan.

Nudge: "You mentioned wanting your life outside work to be 'yours to plan.' What would that look like for you, ideally?"

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
Handed in my notice at Zain last week and everyone keeps asking why I'd leave a senior role with stock options. I don't have a clean answer. Something just goes dead inside me when I know exactly what the next six months will look like.

Nudge: "What does that deadness feel like when it starts?"

Response: It's hard to describe. Like the air gets thick. Anyway, the new place starts in two weeks so I'm focused on that now.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
First week at NovaPay. The stack is Rust and Go, neither of which I've used professionally before, so I've been staying up past midnight going through documentation. The CTO is 31 and talks faster than anyone I've ever met. The whole office runs on this nervous energy that I find either exhilarating or exhausting depending on the hour.

My wife Dina said I looked happier at dinner last night than I have in months. She's probably right. My mother called from Amman and asked if this is really the time to be starting over, at my age, with the kids' school fees what they are. I told her it's a lateral move, which isn't exactly true — I took a pay cut. But she doesn't need to know that right now.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Second entry today because I just got out of the team standup and I need to note this. They gave me ownership of the payment reconciliation service on day four. At Zain it took me eight months to get write access to the production repo.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
Spent the day pair-programming with Farah, one of the junior engineers. She's 26 and already knows Rust better than most people I've worked with. I kept catching myself wanting to impress her with something clever instead of just learning the idioms properly. Old habit. I measure myself against everyone in the room whether I want to or not.

We got the reconciliation pipeline passing all tests by end of day, which felt good. The CTO, Samir, pinged me in Slack to say "nice velocity for week one." I screenshot that. I know it's a small thing but I needed it. At Zain nobody noticed when you shipped. You'd push a feature that handled millions of transactions and the response was silence or a Jira ticket for the next thing.

After work I went to the gym for the first time in weeks. Ran five kilometers on the treadmill and my knees reminded me I'm not thirty anymore. Dina and I took the kids to City Walk for dinner. Layla wanted sushi, Adam wanted burgers, we compromised on a food court. Normal evening. But I was actually present for it instead of mentally rewriting code in my head, which is rare for me during a new project. Maybe that's a good sign.

I told her it's a lateral move, which isn't exactly true — I took a pay cut. But she doesn't need to know that right now.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Second entry today because I just got out of the team standup and I need to note this. They gave me ownership of the payment reconciliation service on day four. At Zain it took me eight months to get write access to the production repo.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
Spent the day pair-programming with Farah, one of the junior engineers. She's 26 and already knows Rust better than most people I've worked with. I kept catching myself wanting to impress her with something clever instead of just learning the idioms properly. Old habit. I measure myself against everyone in the room whether I want to or not.

We got the reconciliation pipeline passing all tests by end of day, which felt good. The CTO, Samir, pinged me in Slack to say "nice velocity for week one." I screenshot that. I know it's a small thing but I needed it. At Zain nobody noticed when you shipped. You'd push a feature that handled millions of transactions and the response was silence or a Jira ticket for the next thing.

After work I went to the gym for the first time in weeks. Ran five kilometers on the treadmill and my knees reminded me I'm not thirty anymore. Dina and I took the kids to City Walk for dinner. Layla wanted sushi, Adam wanted burgers, we compromised on a food court. Normal evening. But I was actually present for it instead of mentally rewriting code in my head, which is rare for me during a new project. Maybe that's a good sign.

[ENTRY t_index=4]

[ENTRY t_index=7]
Performance review cycle started this week and Samir asked everyone to write self-assessments. I spent two hours on mine, rewriting it three times. The first draft was too modest, the second was too aggressive, the third I think landed somewhere honest. I listed the reconciliation service, the test coverage improvements, the documentation I rewrote. Five weeks of work and I already have a page of deliverables. That's the thing about joining somewhere new — everything you do is visible.

Had an odd moment during Friday prayers at the mosque. The imam was talking about the difference between ambition that serves your community and ambition that isolates you. I'm not especially religious but it struck a nerve. My father was a civil engineer in Amman who built the same kinds of bridges for thirty years and was beloved by everyone in the neighborhood. I can't imagine staying anywhere for thirty years. Does that make me restless or just different? He seemed content. I don't know what contentment feels like, honestly. The closest I get is the first few months at a new job when everything is a puzzle and I'm learning at full speed.

Dina read part of my self-assessment over my shoulder and said "you really need them to see you, don't you." She wasn't being mean about it. But it sat with me because she's right. I do need that. I've always needed that and I've never really asked myself why.

First week at NovaPay. The stack is Rust and Go, neither of which I've used professionally before, so I've been staying up past midnight going through documentation. The CTO is 31 and talks faster than anyone I've ever met. The whole office runs on this nervous energy that I find either exhilarating or exhausting depending on the hour.

My wife Dina said I looked happier at dinner last night than I have in months. She's probably right. My mother called from Amman and asked if this is really the time to be starting over, at my age, with the kids' school fees what they are. I told her it's a lateral move, which isn't exactly true — I took a pay cut. But she doesn't need to know that right now.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Second entry today because I just got out of the team standup and I need to note this. They gave me ownership of the payment reconciliation service on day four. At Zain it took me eight months to get write access to the production repo.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
Spent the day pair-programming with Farah, one of the junior engineers. She's 26 and already knows Rust better than most people I've worked with. I kept catching myself wanting to impress her with something clever instead of just learning the idioms properly. Old habit. I measure myself against everyone in the room whether I want to or not.

We got the reconciliation pipeline passing all tests by end of day, which felt good. The CTO, Samir, pinged me in Slack to say "nice velocity for week one." I screenshot that. I know it's a small thing but I needed it. At Zain nobody noticed when you shipped. You'd push a feature that handled millions of transactions and the response was silence or a Jira ticket for the next thing.

After work I went to the gym for the first time in weeks. Ran five kilometers on the treadmill and my knees reminded me I'm not thirty anymore. Dina and I took the kids to City Walk for dinner. Layla wanted sushi, Adam wanted burgers, we compromised on a food court. Normal evening. But I was actually present for it instead of mentally rewriting code in my head, which is rare for me during a new project. Maybe that's a good sign.

[ENTRY t_index=4]
Got invited to speak at a Go meetup next month about the migration patterns we're using at NovaPay. Said yes immediately. Now I have to actually build something worth presenting about, which is either motivating or terrifying, can't tell which.

Nudge: "What made you say yes before thinking it through?"

Response: Because saying no feels like admitting I'm not the kind of person who does these things. And I want to be that person. The one who ships and talks about it and gets known.

[ENTRY t_index=5]
My old colleague Walid messaged me saying NovaPay's last CTO left after six months and the company has a "churn problem." He thinks I made a mistake. I think he's projecting because he's been at the same bank for fifteen years and can't imagine choosing otherwise.

[ENTRY t_index=6]
The meetup talk is in three weeks and I haven't started the slides. I keep opening the deck and then getting pulled into something else — a new Kubernetes operator I want to try, a side project idea for a CLI tool that generates API contracts. My brain refuses to stay on one track. The talk would be good for visibility, I know this, and yet every time I sit down to work on it I feel this resistance like I'm doing homework.

Dina asked tonight if I've made any friends at the new company or if it's "all just code again." Fair question. I eat lunch at my desk most days. There's a group that goes to the shawarma place downstairs but I usually skip it because I'm deep in something. I told her I'll try to go next week. She gave me that look that says she's heard this before.

[ENTRY t_index=7]
Performance review cycle started this week and Samir asked everyone to write self-assessments. I spent two hours on mine, rewriting it three times. The first draft was too modest, the second was too aggressive, the third I think landed somewhere honest. I listed the reconciliation service, the test coverage improvements, the documentation I rewrote. Five weeks of work and I already have a page of deliverables. That's the thing about joining somewhere new — everything you do is visible.

Had an odd moment during Friday prayers at the mosque. The imam was talking about the difference between ambition that serves your community and ambition that isolates you. I'm not especially religious but it struck a nerve. My father was a civil engineer in Amman who built the same kinds of bridges for thirty years and was beloved by everyone in the neighborhood. I can't imagine staying anywhere for thirty years. Does that make me restless or just different? He seemed content. I don't know what contentment feels like, honestly. The closest I get is the first few months at a new job when everything is a puzzle and I'm learning at full speed.

Dina read part of my self-assessment over my shoulder and said "you really need them to see you, don't you." She wasn't being mean about it. But it sat with me because she's right. I do need that. I've always needed that and I've never really asked myself why.

[ENTRY t_index=8]
Gave the Go meetup talk last night. About sixty people showed up. The demo worked — the live migration ran without crashing, which honestly I was sweating about until the last minute. Got solid questions afterward and two people asked for my LinkedIn. Samir was in the audience and mentioned it in the team channel this morning.

Good day. Drove home with the windows down even though it was barely 18 degrees out. Put on Fairuz, which I only listen to when I'm in a specific mood. Dina had kept dinner warm. I told the kids about the talk and Layla said "baba, are you famous now?" which made me laugh more than it should have.

Nudge: "What mattered more — the talk going well or people noticing?"

[ENTRY t_index=9]
Three months into NovaPay. The initial rush is starting to flatten. Not gone, but the problems are becoming familiar. I know the codebase now, I know the deployment pipeline, I know where the bodies are buried. That feeling of every day being a discovery is fading into competence, which should be a good thing but it makes me uneasy.

I keep thinking about a conversation with my uncle Faris last Eid. He runs a carpentry shop in Amman, same shop for forty years. I asked him if he ever gets bored and he looked at me like I'd asked something absurd. He said the wood is different every time — different grain, different knots, different mood. Same tools, same hands, but never the same piece. I want to believe that about software but I'm not sure I do.

Had a one-on-one with Samir and he talked about making me tech lead for the new payments vertical. It's a big deal. More responsibility, more visibility, a path to VP if the company scales. On paper it's everything I've been working toward. But there's a part of me that immediately started thinking about what else is out there. A friend forwarded me a job posting for a robotics startup in Abu Dhabi doing computer vision for warehouse automation. Completely different domain. I bookmarked it and I don't know why. I have a good thing here. Why can't I just stay with it?

Nudge: "What would happen if you stayed this time?"

[ENTRY t_index=10]
Dina found the Abu Dhabi job posting in my browser history. She didn't yell, just got very quiet, which is worse. I told her I was just looking, which is true. Everyone looks.

Nudge: "What was it like when she went quiet?"

Response: Like she was doing math in her head. Calculating how many times we've done this. The kids changed schools twice in four years because of me. I know she's tired of it. I'm tired of it too, honestly. But the looking feels like breathing — I don't know how to stop.

[ENTRY t_index=11]
Second entry today because I can't sleep. It's 1am and I've been lying here thinking about the tech lead offer. Samir wants an answer by Monday. The role is everything that would look right on a CV — leadership, equity, a team of eight reporting to me. My father would have understood this kind of trajectory even if he didn't understand the technology. Build something, get recognized, build more.

But then I picture myself in six months running the same standups, managing the same sprint cycles, dealing with stakeholder calls about roadmap timelines. The operational weight of it. I know the excitement would drain out and I'd be left holding a title that looks impressive but feels like a cage. This has happened before. At Zain, at the logistics company before that, at the consulting firm before that. The pattern is so clear that I could draw it on my whiteboard — six months of fire, six months of friction, then I leave.

I don't know what I actually want. I thought I wanted to prove I could build things that matter and have people notice. And I can. I have. But the moment it becomes routine I start suffocating. And I don't know if the problem is the jobs or if the problem is me. Dina asked me once, years ago, whether I'm running toward something or away from something. I laughed it off. Tonight it doesn't feel funny.

Nudge: "Dina's question — what do you think now?"

Response: I think I'm doing both. Running toward the next thing because it makes me feel alive, and running from the part where being good at something stops being enough and you actually have to sit with yourself. My father could sit with himself. I never learned how. Maybe that's what I'm actually avoiding — not boredom, but stillness.

I'm not especially religious but it struck a nerve. My father was a civil engineer in Amman who built the same kinds of bridges for thirty years and was beloved by everyone in the neighborhood. I can't imagine staying anywhere for thirty years. Does that make me restless or just different? He seemed content. I don't know what contentment feels like, honestly. The closest I get is the first few months at a new job when everything is a puzzle and I'm learning at full speed.

Dina read part of my self-assessment over my shoulder and said "you really need them to see you, don't you." She wasn't being mean about it. But it sat with me because she's right. I do need that. I've always needed that and I've never really asked myself why.

[ENTRY t_index=8]
Gave the Go meetup talk last night. About sixty people showed up. The demo worked — the live migration ran without crashing, which honestly I was sweating about until the last minute. Got solid questions afterward and two people asked for my LinkedIn. Samir was in the audience and mentioned it in the team channel this morning.

Good day. Drove home with the windows down even though it was barely 18 degrees out. Put on Fairuz, which I only listen to when I'm in a specific mood. Dina had kept dinner warm. I told the kids about the talk and Layla said "baba, are you famous now?" which made me laugh more than it should have.

Nudge: "What mattered more — the talk going well or people noticing?"

[ENTRY t_index=9]
Three months into NovaPay. The initial rush is starting to flatten. Not gone, but the problems are becoming familiar. I know the codebase now, I know the deployment pipeline, I know where the bodies are buried. That feeling of every day being a discovery is fading into competence, which should be a good thing but it makes me uneasy.

I keep thinking about a conversation with my uncle Faris last Eid. He runs a carpentry shop in Amman, same shop for forty years. I asked him if he ever gets bored and he looked at me like I'd asked something absurd. He said the wood is different every time — different grain, different knots, different mood. Same tools, same hands, but never the same piece. I want to believe that about software but I'm not sure I do.

Had a one-on-one with Samir and he talked about making me tech lead for the new payments vertical. It's a big deal. More responsibility, more visibility, a path to VP if the company scales. On paper it's everything I've been working toward. But there's a part of me that immediately started thinking about what else is out there. A friend forwarded me a job posting for a robotics startup in Abu Dhabi doing computer vision for warehouse automation. Completely different domain. I bookmarked it and I don't know why. I have a good thing here. Why can't I just stay with it?

Nudge: "What would happen if you stayed this time?"

[ENTRY t_index=10]
Dina found the Abu Dhabi job posting in my browser history. She didn't yell, just got very quiet, which is worse. I told her I was just looking, which is true. Everyone looks.

Nudge: "What was it like when she went quiet?"

Response: Like she was doing math in her head. Calculating how many times we've done this. The kids changed schools twice in four years because of me. I know she's tired of it. I'm tired of it too, honestly. But the looking feels like breathing — I don't know how to stop.

[ENTRY t_index=11]
Second entry today because I can't sleep. It's 1am and I've been lying here thinking about the tech lead offer. Samir wants an answer by Monday. The role is everything that would look right on a CV — leadership, equity, a team of eight reporting to me. My father would have understood this kind of trajectory even if he didn't understand the technology. Build something, get recognized, build more.

But then I picture myself in six months running the same standups, managing the same sprint cycles, dealing with stakeholder calls about roadmap timelines. The operational weight of it. I know the excitement would drain out and I'd be left holding a title that looks impressive but feels like a cage. This has happened before. At Zain, at the logistics company before that, at the consulting firm before that. The pattern is so clear that I could draw it on my whiteboard — six months of fire, six months of friction, then I leave.

I don't know what I actually want. I thought I wanted to prove I could build things that matter and have people notice. And I can. I have. But the moment it becomes routine I start suffocating. And I don't know if the problem is the jobs or if the problem is me. Dina asked me once, years ago, whether I'm running toward something or away from something. I laughed it off. Tonight it doesn't feel funny.

Nudge: "Dina's question — what do you think now?"

Response: I think I'm doing both. Running toward the next thing because it makes me feel alive, and running from the part where being good at something stops being enough and you actually have to sit with yourself. My father could sit with himself. I never learned how. Maybe that's what I'm actually avoiding — not boredom, but stillness.

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
Long shift today—twelve hours on the pediatric oncology floor. Lost a patient, a seven-year-old boy named Diego. His mother was inconsolable, and I stayed an extra hour just sitting with her, holding her hand because that's what my own mother would have done. That's what we do in my family. You don't leave people alone with grief.

I came home and lit a candle at my altar. Said a prayer for Diego, then another for his family. It felt right to do something, even if it's small. The other nurses on my floor, the American ones, they cope by going out for drinks or hitting the gym. That's fine for them. But I need the quiet. I need the ritual. It centers me.

Called Mami afterward. She didn't say much, just listened while I cried a little. Then she reminded me that this work is holy, that caring for the sick is what God asks of us. I don't know if I believe exactly what she believes anymore, but I believe in that feeling—that this job matters, that being present for people in their worst moments is something sacred.

I'm exhausted but I don't regret the extra hour. I'd do it again tomorrow.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Had coffee with my cousin Marisol after work. We talked about maybe getting an apartment together next year—it would cut costs and she's family, so I trust her. It's nice to have someone nearby who understands why I do things the way I do.

Nudge: "What would it mean for you to have Marisol closer in your daily life?"

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Posadas season started this week. The hospital let me organize a small celebration for the pediatric patients who were well enough to participate. We made paper luminarias, sang "Pidiendo Posada," and I brought in tamales that my tía made. Some of the kids had never seen anything like it—they kept asking questions about why we go door to door, why we carry the figures of Mary and Joseph.

Explaining it to them made me realize how much I've been holding onto these things without really thinking about why. It's not just about religion, not really. It's about belonging to something bigger than myself. Something that connects me to my grandmother, my great-grandmother, and all the way back. When I sing those songs, I can hear my abuela's voice mixing with mine, even though she's thousands of miles away.

One of the other nurses, Jessica, said she thought it was "so cool" that I have all these customs. She said her family doesn't really do anything special for the holidays anymore. I felt sad for her. Not in a judgy way, just—I can't imagine not having that thread connecting me to where I come from. It would feel like floating.

I'm already planning what to bring for Nochebuena. The head nurse said I could have Christmas Eve off if I work Christmas Day, which is perfect because Christmas Eve is when my family has always gathered anyway.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
My supervisor offered me a position on the travel nursing team. Better pay, opportunity to see different parts of the country. I told her I'd think about it but I already know my answer.

The money would help—I could send more home, save faster for the condo. But travel nursing means no consistent schedule, no knowing where I'll be month to month. My cousin is counting on me for the apartment plan. And I can't just leave Miami when my whole support system is here. Abuela wasn't thrilled when I left Guatemala; leaving again feels like I'd be proving something wrong.

Nudge: "It sounds like there might be more pulling at you here than just the practical decision. What feels hardest to say out loud?"

Response: The hardest thing to say is that part of me is curious. Part of me wonders what it would be like to just go somewhere new, not have to be the one everyone depends on for a minute. But that's selfish. My family gave up a lot so I could be here. I'm supposed to be building something stable, not chasing adventures. That's not who I am.

[ENTRY t_index=6]
Had an argument with my coworker Rachel today. She made a comment about how I'm "always on the phone with my mom" and said it in a way that felt like she was making fun of me. I told her that in my family, we actually talk to each other, and she got quiet after that. Maybe I was too harsh but I don't regret it.

I know Americans have different relationships with their families. I know not everyone has a mother they can call whenever they need to hear a familiar voice. But the way she said it—like I'm some kind of child who can't function without parental approval—that stung. I'm not asking for permission when I call Mami. I'm asking for connection. There's a difference.

And yes, maybe I do ask her opinion on things more than Rachel asks her mother. So what? My mother has lived through things Rachel can't imagine. She raised four children while my father worked two jobs. She kept our family together when half the neighborhood was falling apart. Her opinion matters to me because she's earned it.

I'm not going to change who I am to make Rachel comfortable. If she thinks I'm too close to my family, that's her problem. I'd rather be "too close" than whatever she has going on where she barely speaks to her parents once a month.

Nudge: "Underneath the frustration, what did Rachel's comment touch in you?"

Response: It touched the part of me that sometimes wonders if I'm holding on too tight. If maybe the way I was raised doesn't fit perfectly here. I don't want to question how my family does things—we've been this way for generations and it's gotten us through everything. But sometimes I feel caught between two worlds, and comments like Rachel's just remind me that I don't fully belong in either one.

[ENTRY t_index=7]
The condo I was looking at fell through. Someone else made a cash offer and the seller took it. Marisol said we could just find another one, but she doesn't get it. That was the building where our other cousins live. That was the whole point.

Everyone keeps telling me it's fine, that there will be other places, but I had planned around this. I had a whole picture in my head: Marisol and me in our place, walking distance from Lucia and her kids, Sunday dinners that don't require an hour of driving. Now I have to start over and I don't even know what I'm looking for anymore.

Now it's New Year's Eve and I'm home early from a short shift. The city is loud with fireworks and parties, but I'm content to stay in. I cleaned my apartment, changed the water for my santos, and made a list of things I want to accomplish next year: get the condo, help Marisol settle in, call home more often, maybe learn to make mole the way my grandmother does.

It's strange to think about the year ending. I feel like I'm exactly where I should be—maybe not where I dreamed as a teenager, but where I'm meant to be. There's comfort in that. Knowing that I'm building something solid, something that will last.

[ENTRY t_index=5]
Started the new year by setting up automatic transfers to my savings account. It's not much, but seeing that number grow a little each month makes me feel like I'm actually getting somewhere. Small steps.

Nudge: "Where is 'somewhere' for you? What does getting there look like?"

[ENTRY t_index=6]
Had an argument with my coworker Rachel today. She made a comment about how I'm "always on the phone with my mom" and said it in a way that felt like she was making fun of me. I told her that in my family, we actually talk to each other, and she got quiet after that. Maybe I was too harsh but I don't regret it.

I know Americans have different relationships with their families. I know not everyone has a mother they can call whenever they need to hear a familiar voice. But the way she said it—like I'm some kind of child who can't function without parental approval—that stung. I'm not asking for permission when I call Mami. I'm asking for connection. There's a difference.

And yes, maybe I do ask her opinion on things more than Rachel asks her mother. So what? My mother has lived through things Rachel can't imagine. She raised four children while my father worked two jobs. She kept our family together when half the neighborhood was falling apart. Her opinion matters to me because she's earned it.

I'm not going to change who I am to make Rachel comfortable. If she thinks I'm too close to my family, that's her problem. I'd rather be "too close" than whatever she has going on where she barely speaks to her parents once a month.

Nudge: "Underneath the frustration, what did Rachel's comment touch in you?"

Response: It touched the part of me that sometimes wonders if I'm holding on too tight. If maybe the way I was raised doesn't fit perfectly here. I don't want to question how my family does things—we've been this way for generations and it's gotten us through everything. But sometimes I feel caught between two worlds, and comments like Rachel's just remind me that I don't fully belong in either one.

[ENTRY t_index=7]
The condo I was looking at fell through. Someone else made a cash offer and the seller took it. Marisol said we could just find another one, but she doesn't get it. That was the building where our other cousins live. That was the whole point.

Everyone keeps telling me it's fine, that there will be other places, but I had planned around this. I had a whole picture in my head: Marisol and me in our place, walking distance from Lucia and her kids, Sunday dinners that don't require an hour of driving. Now I have to start over and I don't even know what I'm looking for anymore.

[ENTRY t_index=8]
I talked to Abuela today. Really talked—not just the usual check-in where we say we're fine and miss each other. I told her about the condo, about feeling stuck, about Rachel and the travel nursing offer and all of it. She listened for a long time without interrupting, which is unusual for her.

Then she told me a story I'd never heard. About when she was young, maybe a little younger than me, and she moved from her village to Guatemala City for work. She said she cried every night for months. She said the city people made fun of how she talked, how she dressed, the food she cooked. She said she thought about going home so many times she lost count.

But she stayed. She stayed because she could see something on the other side of the hard part—a life she could build, children she could raise in a place with more opportunities. She said the crying stopped eventually. Not because it got easier, but because she grew into the person who could handle it.

"Mija," she said, "you're already that person. You just don't see it yet."

I cried after we hung up. Good tears, though. The kind that clean something out. I don't know if I'll find a condo in that building or if Marisol and I will end up somewhere else entirely. I don't know if I'll ever stop feeling caught between here and home. But I know that whatever I build, it will have roots. It will have her in it, and Mami, and everyone who came before.

That's enough. That has to be enough.

Nudge: "What would it look like if it were simply 'enough'—without the 'has to be'?"

Response: I think it would look like trusting that I'm not going to lose anything by being here. That keeping our ways alive doesn't require living in the same building as my cousins or calling Mami every single day. That the roots can stretch without breaking. I'm not there yet. But maybe I'm closer than I was this morning.

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
Long shift today—twelve hours on the pediatric oncology floor. Lost a patient, a seven-year-old boy named Diego. His mother was inconsolable, and I stayed an extra hour just sitting with her, holding her hand because that's what my own mother would have done. That's what we do in my family. You don't leave people alone with grief.

I came home and lit a candle at my altar. Said a prayer for Diego, then another for his family. It felt right to do something, even if it's small. The other nurses on my floor, the American ones, they cope by going out for drinks or hitting the gym. That's fine for them. But I need the quiet. I need the ritual. It centers me.

Called Mami afterward. She didn't say much, just listened while I cried a little. Then she reminded me that this work is holy, that caring for the sick is what God asks of us. I don't know if I believe exactly what she believes anymore, but I believe in that feeling—that this job matters, that being present for people in their worst moments is something sacred.

I'm exhausted but I don't regret the extra hour. I'd do it again tomorrow.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Had coffee with my cousin Marisol after work. We talked about maybe getting an apartment together next year—it would cut costs and she's family, so I trust her. It's nice to have someone nearby who understands why I do things the way I do.

Nudge: "What would it mean for you to have Marisol closer in your daily life?"

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Posadas season started this week. The hospital let me organize a small celebration for the pediatric patients who were well enough to participate. We made paper luminarias, sang "Pidiendo Posada," and I brought in tamales that my tía made. Some of the kids had never seen anything like it—they kept asking questions about why we go door to door, why we carry the figures of Mary and Joseph.

Explaining it to them made me realize how much I've been holding onto these things without really thinking about why. It's not just about religion, not really. It's about belonging to something bigger than myself. Something that connects me to my grandmother, my great-grandmother, and all the way back. When I sing those songs, I can hear my abuela's voice mixing with mine, even though she's thousands of miles away.

One of the other nurses, Jessica, said she thought it was "so cool" that I have all these customs. She said her family doesn't really do anything special for the holidays anymore. I felt sad for her. Not in a judgy way, just—I can't imagine not having that thread connecting me to where I come from. It would feel like floating.

I'm already planning what to bring for Nochebuena. The head nurse said I could have Christmas Eve off if I work Christmas Day, which is perfect because Christmas Eve is when my family has always gathered anyway.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
My supervisor offered me a position on the travel nursing team. Better pay, opportunity to see different parts of the country. I told her I'd think about it but I already know my answer.

The money would help—I could send more home, save faster for the condo. But travel nursing means no consistent schedule, no knowing where I'll be month to month. My cousin is counting on me for the apartment plan. And I can't just leave Miami when my whole support system is here. Abuela wasn't thrilled when I left Guatemala; leaving again feels like I'd be proving something wrong.

Nudge: "It sounds like there might be more pulling at you here than just the practical decision. What feels hardest to say out loud?"

Response: The hardest thing to say is that part of me is curious. Part of me wonders what it would be like to just go somewhere new, not have to be the one everyone depends on for a minute. But that's selfish. My family gave up a lot so I could be here. I'm supposed to be building something stable, not chasing adventures. That's not who I am.

[ENTRY t_index=0]
Marking forty-two exam papers and my eyes are giving out. Priya from Class 10 wrote something genuinely moving about her grandmother's village, but I cannot even enjoy it properly because the stack keeps growing and dinner still needs sorting.

Mummy-ji asked twice when I would be done with the table because she wants to lay out her medicines. I said ten more minutes both times and it was never ten minutes. Rajesh heated leftovers for everyone without being asked, which was something. Still at the table now. Still marking.

Nudge: "Who got the short end tonight?"

Response: Everyone, probably. The papers got half my attention, Mummy-ji got empty promises, and I did not even sit with Rajesh while he ate.

[ENTRY t_index=0]
Marking forty-two exam papers and my eyes are giving out. Priya from Class 10 wrote something genuinely moving about her grandmother's village, but I cannot even enjoy it properly because the stack keeps growing and dinner still needs sorting.

Mummy-ji asked twice when I would be done with the table because she wants to lay out her medicines. I said ten more minutes both times and it was never ten minutes. Rajesh heated leftovers for everyone without being asked, which was something. Still at the table now. Still marking.

Nudge: "Who got the short end tonight?"

Response: Everyone, probably. The papers got half my attention, Mummy-ji got empty promises, and I did not even sit with Rajesh while he ate.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Parent-teacher meeting ran an hour over. Sharmaji spent twenty minutes arguing his son deserves higher marks in poetry analysis. I explained the rubric twice. He was not satisfied but eventually left.

Came home to find the washing machine had leaked again. Rajesh called the repair person. Bahu made chai without asking, which was nice. Ate late. Nothing else to say about it.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Same day but lying in bed now and actually feeling lighter than expected. The chai Bahu made — she added elaichi the way I like it without me mentioning. Small thing but it sat with me.

Nudge: "What made that land so much?"

Response: She noticed. Nobody asked her to, she just paid attention. Sometimes that is the whole thing.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
Ritu called during lunch break, which never happens on weekdays, and for a second my chest seized because I thought something was wrong with the baby. But no — she just wanted to tell me that little Aarav said "Nani" for the first time. Clear as anything, she said, not a babble. She held the phone to him and he did it again and I was standing in the staff room corridor pressing the phone to my ear like an idiot with tears running.

The other teachers walked past and Sunita asked if everything was okay. I just nodded and waved her off. Could not explain it properly. Twenty-eight years of students calling me Meena ma'am and none of it hits the way one small boy saying Nani does.

Came home and told Mummy-ji and she got teary too. We sat together for a bit, just the two of us, which does not happen often these days because the house is always full of someone needing something. Rajesh came in and saw us and quietly backed out. He knows when to leave a room. That is its own kind of gift, knowing when not to interrupt.

[ENTRY t_index=4]
Spent the morning helping Sunita draft her transfer application — she wants to move closer to her aging parents in Varanasi. Felt right, sitting with her and getting the wording correct, even though it means the department loses a good teacher.

Nudge: "You're helping her leave. Does that sting at all?"

Response: It is her parents, what else would I tell her. Anyway the application needed better phrasing, that was the immediate problem.

[ENTRY t_index=6]

Nudge: "Who got the short end tonight?"

[ENTRY t_index=1]

[ENTRY t_index=2]

[ENTRY t_index=3]
Ritu called during lunch break, which never happens on weekdays, and for a second my chest seized because I thought something was wrong with the baby. Came home and told Mummy-ji and she got teary too. We sat together for a bit, just the two of us, which does not happen often these days because the house is always full of someone needing something. [ENTRY t_index=0]
My sister-in-law made a comment at dinner about how she could never "just stay home." I smiled and said something about how it's not for everyone. Dan didn't say anything. Nobody said anything. I loaded the dishwasher after and just stood there gripping the counter.

Nudge: "What were you gripping the counter about?"

Response: I wanted to say something back. Something sharp. But that would've made dinner weird and Dan's mom was right there. So I just didn't. And now it's stuck in my chest.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
I know people think staying home is easy or that I'm wasting my degree. I see it in the way my old coworkers talk to me at birthday parties, like they're being careful not to make me feel bad about my choices. But honestly, I don't think they could do what I do. Managing two kids, the house, Dan's travel schedule, appointments, groceries, meal prep, all of it on a rotating loop that never pauses.

And yeah, I chose this. Nobody forced me. When I was pregnant with Owen we sat down and looked at daycare costs and my salary and it didn't make sense. Dan makes more. That's just math. And I wanted to be home with them during these early years, I really did. I still do. But wanting something and never hearing anyone say "good job" or "thank you" are two different things.

My mom called this morning and asked if I'd thought about going back to work part-time. I told her I'm fine. She does this every few months, like she's checking if I've come to my senses. I know she means well. I just wish people would stop treating my life like a problem to solve.

Nudge: "What would 'good job' look like coming from someone?"

Response: I don't even know. Maybe Dan noticing that the house runs because I make it run. Or my mom saying she's proud of me without the follow-up question. But I think the real thing is I want to believe it myself. That this counts. That I'm not just filling time until the kids start school and I have to figure out who I am again.

Nudge: "What would 'good job' look like coming from someone?"

But I think the real thing is I want to believe it myself. That this counts. That I'm not just filling time until the kids start school and I have to figure out who I am again.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Playgroup today. One of the other moms asked my opinion on the new park hours and I just agreed with whatever she said. Drove home wondering why I do that. It wasn't even something worth disagreeing about but I noticed myself doing it anyway.

Nudge: "What would have happened if you'd disagreed?"

Response: Probably nothing. She would've shrugged and moved on. But in my head it felt like it would've been this whole thing. Like I'd be the difficult one. I know that's ridiculous but it's automatic.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
Dan's mom called this morning to ask if we're coming to Sunday dinner. I said yes. She asked if I could bring a salad and the rolls, same as always. I said sure. She mentioned that Dan's brother and his wife are bringing their new baby, so it'll be a full house. I said that sounds nice.

After I hung up I looked at my calendar. Sunday is the only day Dan is home this week. I had been thinking about asking him if we could skip dinner and just do something as a family, the four of us. Take the kids to the aquarium or even just stay home and order pizza. But I already said yes. And backing out would be a thing. His mom would call Dan, Dan would ask me what happened, and I'd have to explain that I just didn't want to go, which sounds selfish when there's a new baby to meet and everyone will be there.

So we'll go. I'll make the salad and the rolls. The kids will run around with their cousins and it'll be fine. It's always fine. I just noticed that I committed us before I even thought about what I wanted. Like my mouth answered before my brain caught up.

Nudge: "What would you have wanted instead?"

Response: Just a quiet day. Nothing dramatic. But it's fine, really. The kids love seeing their cousins.

[ENTRY t_index=4]
Same day, later. Dan called to check in and I mentioned Sunday dinner and he said "oh great, I was hoping we'd go." And something in me just deflated. Not because he did anything wrong. He didn't know I wanted something different because I never said it. I never say it.

I keep replaying the moment I picked up the phone with his mom. I could have said let me check with Dan first. That's a normal thing to say. But I didn't because saying that would've felt like making waves. And I can't stand making waves even when the water is perfectly calm and nobody would even notice.

I'm tired of being the person who says yes to everything and then sits alone in the kitchen feeling resentful about it.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Ran the quarterly review myself today, no slides from anyone else, just my numbers, my narrative. The room was quiet in that good way where people are actually listening. Felt like the division is finally mine in the way I wanted it to be.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Needed sign-off from Legal on the new supplier contract. Straightforward, or so I thought. Sent it Thursday, followed up Friday morning, got told Anke was "reviewing priorities" and would get to it when she could. Meanwhile the supplier's deadline is Tuesday and I'm the one who'll look like I dropped the ball.

It's not even about the contract, really. It's that I had to sit there refreshing my inbox like some junior waiting for permission. I run a forty-three person division and I can't move a standard supplier agreement through because someone in a different department decides the timeline. I almost went to the CEO directly but thought better of it. Probably should have.

Nudge: "Why did you hold back from going to the CEO?"

Monday. Staff meeting went long because Thomas wanted to discuss the new reporting structure again. I'd already decided on it two weeks ago and communicated it clearly, but apparently some people need to re-litigate everything. Let them talk for ten minutes, then moved on.

Lotte asked how work was over dinner. I said fine. She gave me that look, the one that means she knows I'm not actually answering. [ENTRY t_index=4]
Monday. Staff meeting went long because Thomas wanted to discuss the new reporting structure again. I'd already decided on it two weeks ago and communicated it clearly, but apparently some people need to re-litigate everything. Let them talk for ten minutes, then moved on.

Lotte asked how work was over dinner. I said fine. She gave me that look, the one that means she knows I'm not actually answering. I wasn't trying to be evasive, I just genuinely didn't have the energy to translate the day into something she'd find interesting. Watched the football afterward, fell asleep on the couch again.

[ENTRY t_index=5]
Had a clear plan for the fleet optimization rollout. Presented it in the ops meeting. Sandra pushed back hard, said we should phase it differently, go region by region instead of all at once. Others started nodding along with her, and I could feel the room shifting.

I ended up agreeing to her phased approach. It's not wrong, technically. But it wasn't my call anymore, and everyone in that room saw it happen. I keep telling myself it doesn't matter how we get there as long as we get there. Not sure I believe that.

Nudge: "What exactly changed when the room shifted to Sandra's side?"

Response: It wasn't about the plan. It was that they looked at her like she was in charge of the decision.

[ENTRY t_index=6]
Sunday but I can't stop thinking about Friday. New hire, Jeroen, fresh from his MBA, has been here eight weeks. In the project kickoff he interrupted me to "build on" my point, then spent five minutes explaining container throughput metrics to me like I haven't been running this operation for a decade. The room just let it happen. Nobody stepped in.

What gets me is that I know this kid's type. He's positioning. He's already had coffee with the CEO twice, which I only know because I saw it in the calendar. And I just sat there and let him reframe my own analysis back to me. I should have shut it down. I should have said, "Jeroen, I've been doing this since before you started your bachelor's." But I didn't. I smiled and nodded like it was a collaborative discussion.

I keep thinking about what my father would say. He ran his own business for thirty years and nobody ever talked over him because he made sure of it from day one. I don't know when I started letting people do this to me. Maybe it's the restructure, maybe I'm spread too thin to fight every battle. But it feels like something I'm losing and I can't quite name what it is.

Nudge: "What is it you think you're losing?"

Response: The room's assumption that I'm the one who decides.

[ENTRY t_index=8]

Spent an hour going through old emails, not for work reasons, just sorting. Found some from when I first got promoted to division head. I was so careful in those early messages, checking every word. Now I fire things off without thinking. Not sure if that's confidence or just not caring as much.

[ENTRY t_index=9]
Had a one-on-one with Lisa from my team today. She's been struggling with the new reporting system and came in looking like she expected to be reprimanded. I didn't do that. Just walked through it with her, showed her the shortcuts I use, told her it took me three months to stop hating the interface too. She actually laughed.

Small thing, but it reminded me of something. When people come to me for direction and I give it clearly, no games, no politics, just here's how we do this — that's when I feel most like myself. Not the boardroom stuff, not the jockeying. Just someone who knows what they're doing, passing it on. Drove home in a decent mood for once.

[ENTRY t_index=10]
Board prep meeting today. The CFO asked me to "walk him through" my division's numbers as if I hadn't already submitted a forty-page deck last week. He does this to me every quarter. He doesn't do it to Pieter, doesn't do it to Sandra. Just me. Like I need to audition for the right to present my own results.

I complied, obviously. Went through it line by line, smiled, answered his follow-ups. Drove home and sat in the car in the garage for ten minutes before going inside. Lotte asked if I was okay and I said yes. I wasn't really.

Nudge: "What were you thinking about in those ten minutes in the car?"

Response: Just decompressing. Long day.

[ENTRY t_index=0]
Pieter talked right over me in the steering committee today, mid-sentence, and presented the container routing proposal like it was his. I just sat there. Too tired to fight it, too angry to let it go, and now it's midnight and I'm still turning it over.

Nudge: "What stopped you from calling it out right there?"

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Ran the quarterly review myself today, no slides from anyone else, just my numbers, my narrative. The room was quiet in that good way where people are actually listening. Felt like the division is finally mine in the way I wanted it to be.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Needed sign-off from Legal on the new supplier contract. Straightforward, or so I thought. Sent it Thursday, followed up Friday morning, got told Anke was "reviewing priorities" and would get to it when she could. Meanwhile the supplier's deadline is Tuesday and I'm the one who'll look like I dropped the ball.

It's not even about the contract, really. It's that I had to sit there refreshing my inbox like some junior waiting for permission. I run a forty-three person division and I can't move a standard supplier agreement through because someone in a different department decides the timeline. I almost went to the CEO directly but thought better of it. Probably should have.

Nudge: "Why did you hold back from going to the CEO?"

[ENTRY t_index=3]
Routine day. Back-to-back calls, budget reconciliation, nothing unusual. Grabbed a broodje kroket from the canteen and ate it at my desk between meetings.

[ENTRY t_index=4]
Monday. Staff meeting went long because Thomas wanted to discuss the new reporting structure again. I'd already decided on it two weeks ago and communicated it clearly, but apparently some people need to re-litigate everything. Let them talk for ten minutes, then moved on.

Lotte asked how work was over dinner. I said fine. She gave me that look, the one that means she knows I'm not actually answering. I wasn't trying to be evasive, I just genuinely didn't have the energy to translate the day into something she'd find interesting. Watched the football afterward, fell asleep on the couch again.

[ENTRY t_index=5]
Had a clear plan for the fleet optimization rollout. Presented it in the ops meeting. Sandra pushed back hard, said we should phase it differently, go region by region instead of all at once. Others started nodding along with her, and I could feel the room shifting.

I ended up agreeing to her phased approach. It's not wrong, technically. But it wasn't my call anymore, and everyone in that room saw it happen. I keep telling myself it doesn't matter how we get there as long as we get there. Not sure I believe that.

Nudge: "What exactly changed when the room shifted to Sandra's side?"

Response: It wasn't about the plan. It was that they looked at her like she was in charge of the decision.

[ENTRY t_index=6]
Sunday but I can't stop thinking about Friday. New hire, Jeroen, fresh from his MBA, has been here eight weeks. In the project kickoff he interrupted me to "build on" my point, then spent five minutes explaining container throughput metrics to me like I haven't been running this operation for a decade. The room just let it happen. Nobody stepped in.

What gets me is that I know this kid's type. He's positioning. He's already had coffee with the CEO twice, which I only know because I saw it in the calendar. And I just sat there and let him reframe my own analysis back to me. I should have shut it down. I should have said, "Jeroen, I've been doing this since before you started your bachelor's." But I didn't. I smiled and nodded like it was a collaborative discussion.

I keep thinking about what my father would say. He ran his own business for thirty years and nobody ever talked over him because he made sure of it from day one. I don't know when I started letting people do this to me. Maybe it's the restructure, maybe I'm spread too thin to fight every battle. But it feels like something I'm losing and I can't quite name what it is.

Nudge: "What is it you think you're losing?"

Response: The room's assumption that I'm the one who decides.

[ENTRY t_index=7]
Pieter did it again in the client call. Spoke over me, redirected the conversation to his numbers. I corrected him this time but it came out sharper than I meant and the client noticed.

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
My mother-in-law came by today and made some comment about the stack of translation documents on the kitchen table. Something about how I should focus on the kids instead of "outside projects." I smiled and said I'd clear them after lunch. But it sat with me the rest of the afternoon.

She doesn't understand what those documents are. Each one is someone's asylum application or work permit or medical form that they can't read because nobody thought to print it in a language they actually speak. I can do that. I can sit at my kitchen table after the kids are asleep and make that one thing less terrifying for someone. And my kids are fed, they're healthy, they're fine. I don't need to justify this to anyone, but I keep feeling like I have to.

Nudge: "You said you don't need to justify it. So why does it still sit with you?"

Response: Because she's not wrong that there are only so many hours. I chose to stay home for the kids. But the work I left behind didn't stop needing to be done. I just can't shake the feeling that I'm supposed to pick one and be at peace with it, and I haven't figured out how to do that yet.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Residents association meeting tonight about the new recycling sorting rules the district is rolling out. I volunteered to run the information session for our building because nobody else would and the flyer they sent was confusing — mixing up the plastic codes and getting the compost pickup schedule wrong. Spent two hours after the kids' bedtime redoing the flyer in Korean and English since we have several non-Korean families in the complex.

Hyun-woo got home late again and asked why I was still up. I showed him the flyer and he said "can't someone else do this?" He wasn't being mean. He just doesn't get why I'd spend a free evening on something that could theoretically be someone else's problem.

Nudge: "What would happen if you'd just left the flyer as it was?"

Response: The wrong information would go out and our building would get fined again, and the two Filipina families on the fourth floor who can barely read the Korean notices would have no idea what changed. I keep thinking about Mrs. Reyes carrying her bags to the wrong bin last month looking confused and embarrassed. Nobody helped her. I can't see that and not do something. It physically bothers me to walk past a problem I could fix.

It physically bothers me to walk past a problem I could fix.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Took the kids to the park near the river this morning. Eun-bi wanted to feed the ducks and Jun-ho wanted to throw rocks, so we compromised — ducks first, then rocks at a different spot. Standard negotiation. It was actually nice until I noticed a group of volunteers doing a river cleanup downstream. They had those grabber tools and black trash bags and matching vests from some environmental group I didn't recognize.

I stood there watching them for probably five minutes while the kids played. Part of me wanted to walk over and ask how to join. I used to do cleanups like that every month before Jun-ho was born. Our old NGO partnered with a river conservation group in Mapo-gu and I'd spend whole Saturdays hauling plastic out of the Cheonggyecheon tributaries. Felt like I was doing something tangible, something I could point at and say today this stretch of water is cleaner because I was here.

Now I watch from the playground. I'm not complaining about my life — the kids are wonderful and I chose this. But there's this gap between what I used to be able to do and what my days look like now. I don't know what to do with that gap except notice it and move on. Jun-ho threw a rock into the water and it made a perfect splash and he was so proud. I clapped and said "good one" and we went home for lunch.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
Can't sleep. Kept thinking about the river cleanup people. Found their group online — they meet every Saturday, which is the one morning Hyun-woo is reliably home with the kids. I could go. But then I'd be giving up the only morning I have for grocery shopping, meal prep, and the laundry pile that's been staring at me all week.

My friend Ji-yeon texted asking if I wanted to join her yoga class on Saturday mornings. Everyone keeps offering me ways to spend my time that are about me. Yoga, self-care, "you need a break." But I don't want a break. I want to be useful beyond these walls. That probably sounds ungrateful. I have a warm apartment and two healthy children and a husband who comes home every night. But I keep reading the news about the factory fire in Gimpo where those migrant workers were locked in because the exits were blocked and I can't just — I can't just do yoga about it.

[ENTRY t_index=4]
Went to the refugee resettlement office for my translation shift. They had a backlog of about forty documents — medical intake forms, mostly. One was for a family from Myanmar with three kids under ten. The mother had written additional notes in the margins in Burmese that I couldn't read, so I flagged them for the Burmese translator. Just small handwritten lines. Probably questions she was too afraid to ask out loud. That kind of thing breaks something in me every time.

Came home and Eun-bi had drawn a picture of our family at school. She drew me holding a big stack of papers. Hyun-woo laughed and said "even she knows." I laughed too but it landed weird. Like even my six-year-old sees me as the woman with the papers instead of — I don't know. I don't know what I expected her to draw. I am the woman with the papers. I just wish that didn't feel like it needed defending.

Nudge: "What did you want her to draw instead?"

[ENTRY t_index=5]
Both kids had fevers last night so I was up every two hours with wet towels and children's Tylenol. Jun-ho finally slept at 4am. I skipped the recycling committee meeting and I feel guilty about it even though I know that's absurd.

Nudge: "Why does skipping it feel like a failure?"

Response: Because I told Mrs. Park I'd present the updated sorting schedule and now she has to scramble. I know she'll manage. I know my kids come first. But letting someone down — even for a completely valid reason — just eats at me. It always has.

[ENTRY t_index=6]
Kids are better. Jun-ho's fever broke around noon and Eun-bi was already bouncing off the walls by breakfast. Hyun-woo took the afternoon off which almost never happens, so I had about three hours to myself. I should have napped. Instead I sat on the balcony with my laptop and read through the news.

There's a piece about a proposed bill that would make it harder for undocumented workers to access emergency medical care. I read it twice. Then I started drafting a letter to our local representative, citing cases I'd seen at the resettlement office — anonymized, of course. I wrote about the Myanmar family's intake forms and the gap between what the system promises and what reaches people. It took me two hours and I don't even know if anyone will read it.

Hyun-woo came in and saw me typing and said nothing. He just made me tea and left it on the desk. I think he's given up trying to understand why I spend my rare free hours this way. He's not angry. He's just resigned. And I can feel the distance in that resignation. Like he's accepted that this is who I am but he'd prefer a version of me who could sit on the balcony and just look at the sky. Some days I'd prefer that version too. But I open the news and there's another factory fire or another deportation story or another kid who fell through the cracks and I can't look at the sky. The sky is not the problem.

[ENTRY t_index=7]
Sent the letter. Also emailed the river cleanup group about joining their Saturday rotation starting August. Hyun-woo agreed to handle Saturday mornings. Feels like a small step but at least it's forward.
