CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
My mother-in-law came by today and made some comment about the stack of translation documents on the kitchen table. Something about how I should focus on the kids instead of "outside projects." I smiled and said I'd clear them after lunch. But it sat with me the rest of the afternoon.

She doesn't understand what those documents are. Each one is someone's asylum application or work permit or medical form that they can't read because nobody thought to print it in a language they actually speak. I can do that. I can sit at my kitchen table after the kids are asleep and make that one thing less terrifying for someone. And my kids are fed, they're healthy, they're fine. I don't need to justify this to anyone, but I keep feeling like I have to.

Nudge: "You said you don't need to justify it. So why does it still sit with you?"

Response: Because she's not wrong that there are only so many hours. I chose to stay home for the kids. But the work I left behind didn't stop needing to be done. I just can't shake the feeling that I'm supposed to pick one and be at peace with it, and I haven't figured out how to do that yet.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Residents association meeting tonight about the new recycling sorting rules the district is rolling out. I volunteered to run the information session for our building because nobody else would and the flyer they sent was confusing — mixing up the plastic codes and getting the compost pickup schedule wrong. Spent two hours after the kids' bedtime redoing the flyer in Korean and English since we have several non-Korean families in the complex.

Hyun-woo got home late again and asked why I was still up. I showed him the flyer and he said "can't someone else do this?" He wasn't being mean. He just doesn't get why I'd spend a free evening on something that could theoretically be someone else's problem.

Nudge: "What would happen if you'd just left the flyer as it was?"

Response: The wrong information would go out and our building would get fined again, and the two Filipina families on the fourth floor who can barely read the Korean notices would have no idea what changed. I keep thinking about Mrs. Reyes carrying her bags to the wrong bin last month looking confused and embarrassed. Nobody helped her. I can't see that and not do something. It physically bothers me to walk past a problem I could fix.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Took the kids to the park near the river this morning. Eun-bi wanted to feed the ducks and Jun-ho wanted to throw rocks, so we compromised — ducks first, then rocks at a different spot. Standard negotiation. It was actually nice until I noticed a group of volunteers doing a river cleanup downstream. They had those grabber tools and black trash bags and matching vests from some environmental group I didn't recognize.

I stood there watching them for probably five minutes while the kids played. Part of me wanted to walk over and ask how to join. I used to do cleanups like that every month before Jun-ho was born. Our old NGO partnered with a river conservation group in Mapo-gu and I'd spend whole Saturdays hauling plastic out of the Cheonggyecheon tributaries. Felt like I was doing something tangible, something I could point at and say today this stretch of water is cleaner because I was here.

Now I watch from the playground. I'm not complaining about my life — the kids are wonderful and I chose this. But there's this gap between what I used to be able to do and what my days look like now. I don't know what to do with that gap except notice it and move on. Jun-ho threw a rock into the water and it made a perfect splash and he was so proud. I clapped and said "good one" and we went home for lunch.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
Can't sleep. Kept thinking about the river cleanup people. Found their group online — they meet every Saturday, which is the one morning Hyun-woo is reliably home with the kids. I could go. But then I'd be giving up the only morning I have for grocery shopping, meal prep, and the laundry pile that's been staring at me all week.

My friend Ji-yeon texted asking if I wanted to join her yoga class on Saturday mornings. Everyone keeps offering me ways to spend my time that are about me. Yoga, self-care, "you need a break." But I don't want a break. I want to be useful beyond these walls. That probably sounds ungrateful. I have a warm apartment and two healthy children and a husband who comes home every night. But I keep reading the news about the factory fire in Gimpo where those migrant workers were locked in because the exits were blocked and I can't just — I can't just do yoga about it.

[ENTRY t_index=4]
Went to the refugee resettlement office for my translation shift. They had a backlog of about forty documents — medical intake forms, mostly. One was for a family from Myanmar with three kids under ten. The mother had written additional notes in the margins in Burmese that I couldn't read, so I flagged them for the Burmese translator. Just small handwritten lines. Probably questions she was too afraid to ask out loud. That kind of thing breaks something in me every time.

Came home and Eun-bi had drawn a picture of our family at school. She drew me holding a big stack of papers. Hyun-woo laughed and said "even she knows." I laughed too but it landed weird. Like even my six-year-old sees me as the woman with the papers instead of — I don't know. I don't know what I expected her to draw. I am the woman with the papers. I just wish that didn't feel like it needed defending.

Nudge: "What did you want her to draw instead?"

[ENTRY t_index=7]

[ENTRY t_index=8]

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
Finished a long translation project today for that small organization documenting water access issues in South Asia. The pay was honestly below market rate but reading through those testimonies, I kept thinking about my grandmother's village where they still walk two kilometers for clean water. Some things matter more than what shows up in my bank account.

My cousin called from Mumbai asking why I don't take more corporate work since my English is good enough. I tried explaining but it felt like talking to a wall. She thinks I'm being foolish, giving away my skills to people who can't pay properly. Maybe she's right about the practical side. But when I close my laptop after a day like today, I feel something she probably wouldn't understand.

[ENTRY t_index=0]
Finished a long translation project today for that small organization documenting water access issues in South Asia. The pay was honestly below market rate but reading through those testimonies, I kept thinking about my grandmother's village where they still walk two kilometers for clean water. Some things matter more than what shows up in my bank account.

My cousin called from Mumbai asking why I don't take more corporate work since my English is good enough. I tried explaining but it felt like talking to a wall. She thinks I'm being foolish, giving away my skills to people who can't pay properly. Maybe she's right about the practical side. But when I close my laptop after a day like today, I feel something she probably wouldn't understand.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
The community garden is finally showing real growth. We planted those tomato seedlings with the Syrian family three weeks ago and today Amira brought her daughters to see the little green fruits forming. The oldest one, maybe seven years old, touched the leaves so gently like she was afraid of breaking something precious. I remember that feeling from when I first gardened with my mother on our tiny balcony.

These moments remind me why I spend my Saturdays here instead of picking up extra delivery shifts. Not everything needs a transaction attached to it. Though I'll admit the rent notice sitting on my kitchen counter is making that philosophy harder to hold onto this week. The landlord raised it again and I'm already stretched thin. Still, watching those girls discover where food actually comes from felt like enough today.

Nudge: "You mentioned the rent increase is making your philosophy 'harder to hold onto.' What happens when these two parts of your life feel like they're pulling against each other?"

Response: It terrifies me, honestly. I came here to build a different kind of life than the one that was mapped out for me in Mumbai, where everything is about climbing and earning and comparing. But now I lie awake some nights wondering if I'm just being naive. If my principles are a luxury I can't actually afford. My father used to say that ideals don't fill stomachs, and I hated hearing it, but lately his voice echoes louder. I don't want to become someone who stops caring about the bigger picture just to secure my own small corner. But I also can't keep pretending the practical doesn't matter.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Something small happened during a delivery today that I can't stop thinking about. I was dropping off food at one of those new condo towers downtown, the kind with the marble lobby and the doorman who barely looks at you. The customer came down herself instead of asking me to leave it at the desk. She saw my reusable bag and we ended up talking for five minutes about plastic waste in the ocean. She works for an environmental law firm apparently.

It's easy to feel invisible doing delivery work, just a body moving packages from one place to another. But for those five minutes I was a person with thoughts and concerns that mattered to someone. She gave me her card and said they sometimes need Gujarati translators for community outreach. I probably won't follow up because I'm always hesitant to mix networking with genuine connection. But it reminded me that caring about the world isn't as rare as it sometimes feels. Other people are thinking about these things too.

Nudge: "You said you probably won't follow up because of mixing networking with genuine connection. What feels complicated about that?"

Response: I worry that if I reach out for work, it changes what that conversation was. It becomes transactional instead of just two people recognizing something shared. Maybe that's too precious about it. In Mumbai, everyone networks constantly and there's no shame in it. But I came here partly to escape that feeling of every relationship being measured by what it can do for you. If I contact her for the translation work, am I being practical or am I becoming the thing I was running from?

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
Finished a long translation project today for that small organization documenting water access issues in South Asia. The pay was honestly below market rate but reading through those testimonies, I kept thinking about my grandmother's village where they still walk two kilometers for clean water. Some things matter more than what shows up in my bank account.

My cousin called from Mumbai asking why I don't take more corporate work since my English is good enough. I tried explaining but it felt like talking to a wall. She thinks I'm being foolish, giving away my skills to people who can't pay properly. Maybe she's right about the practical side. But when I close my laptop after a day like today, I feel something she probably wouldn't understand.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
The community garden is finally showing real growth. We planted those tomato seedlings with the Syrian family three weeks ago and today Amira brought her daughters to see the little green fruits forming. The oldest one, maybe seven years old, touched the leaves so gently like she was afraid of breaking something precious. I remember that feeling from when I first gardened with my mother on our tiny balcony.

These moments remind me why I spend my Saturdays here instead of picking up extra delivery shifts. Not everything needs a transaction attached to it. Though I'll admit the rent notice sitting on my kitchen counter is making that philosophy harder to hold onto this week. The landlord raised it again and I'm already stretched thin. Still, watching those girls discover where food actually comes from felt like enough today.

Nudge: "You mentioned the rent increase is making your philosophy 'harder to hold onto.' What happens when these two parts of your life feel like they're pulling against each other?"

Response: It terrifies me, honestly. I came here to build a different kind of life than the one that was mapped out for me in Mumbai, where everything is about climbing and earning and comparing. But now I lie awake some nights wondering if I'm just being naive. If my principles are a luxury I can't actually afford. My father used to say that ideals don't fill stomachs, and I hated hearing it, but lately his voice echoes louder. I don't want to become someone who stops caring about the bigger picture just to secure my own small corner. But I also can't keep pretending the practical doesn't matter.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Something small happened during a delivery today that I can't stop thinking about. I was dropping off food at one of those new condo towers downtown, the kind with the marble lobby and the doorman who barely looks at you. The customer came down herself instead of asking me to leave it at the desk. She saw my reusable bag and we ended up talking for five minutes about plastic waste in the ocean. She works for an environmental law firm apparently.

It's easy to feel invisible doing delivery work, just a body moving packages from one place to another. But for those five minutes I was a person with thoughts and concerns that mattered to someone. She gave me her card and said they sometimes need Gujarati translators for community outreach. I probably won't follow up because I'm always hesitant to mix networking with genuine connection. But it reminded me that caring about the world isn't as rare as it sometimes feels. Other people are thinking about these things too.

Nudge: "You said you probably won't follow up because of mixing networking with genuine connection. What feels complicated about that?"

Response: I worry that if I reach out for work, it changes what that conversation was. It becomes transactional instead of just two people recognizing something shared. Maybe that's too precious about it. In Mumbai, everyone networks constantly and there's no shame in it. But I came here partly to escape that feeling of every relationship being measured by what it can do for you. If I contact her for the translation work, am I being practical or am I becoming the thing I was running from?

[ENTRY t_index=3]
Read an article this morning about garment factory conditions in Bangladesh that ruined my whole day. I know I can't save everyone. I know my choices, even the careful ones about what I buy and who I work for, are drops in an ocean. But sometimes the weight of knowing what's happening out there feels unbearable. All these people suffering right now while I sit here with my morning chai worrying about my rent.

Called my mother after because I needed to hear her voice. She asked why I sounded sad and I couldn't explain. How do you tell someone that you're grieving for people you've never met, that reading about injustice thousands of kilometers away can sink your whole day? She would tell me to focus on my own life, that I can't carry the world. She means well. But the world is not something outside me that I can choose to put down. We're all connected, whether we see it or not.

[ENTRY t_index=4]
Evening now and I'm feeling better. Went back to the garden this afternoon and helped plant cucumber seedlings with Mr. Chen, the retired schoolteacher from the housing complex. He doesn't speak much English but we've developed a language through pointing and smiling and showing each other what to do next. There's something deeply right about working the soil with someone in complete silence.

I think this is what keeps me grounded when the news gets too heavy. Not fixing anything, not solving the big problems, just being present with another person doing something small and generative. The cucumbers will grow whether or not I read about suffering. The children will eat them. And somehow that feels like an answer, even if I can't quite articulate what question it's answering.

Nudge: "You said this feels like an answer to a question you can't articulate. If you tried to put that question into words, what might it sound like?"

Response: I don't know. Maybe I'm just being overly philosophical about putting seeds in dirt. Some things don't need to be analyzed, they just need to be done. My grandmother would say I think too much, that the hands should be busy while the mind rests. Perhaps she was wiser than all my searching.

[ENTRY t_index=5]
Third entry today, I know. But I wanted to capture this before I sleep. Amira, the Syrian mother from the garden, sent me a photo through our community group chat. It's her family eating dinner together, and on the table are tomatoes from our garden. Just regular tomatoes, slightly misshapen, nothing special. But the message she wrote said "Thank you for teaching us this. My daughters now understand that food is a gift."

Sometimes I question whether anything I do matters against the scale of problems in the world. The displacement, the inequality, the destruction of the planet. What difference does one small garden make? But then a photo arrives and I remember that we don't measure impact only in statistics. One family eating vegetables they grew themselves. One child learning that nurturing something is possible. These are not solutions to systemic problems, I know that. But maybe they're something else that also matters. I'm grateful tonight. Even with the uncertainty about rent and whether I'm living foolishly. This is the life I chose.

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
Had my quarterly review today. My director said I "exceeded expectations" and hinted at a senior manager role opening up in Q2. I should feel relieved—this is exactly what I've been working toward for two years. And I do feel good about it. But there's this part of me that immediately started calculating: if I get promoted, how long until I'm considered for the director track? It's like the goalpost moves the second I reach it.

I called Baba tonight to tell him. He was proud, I could hear it in his voice even though he just said "Alhamdulillah, keep your head down and work hard." That's his way. I think he worries I'll get complacent. I won't.

Nudge: "What would reaching the 'final' goalpost actually look like for you?"

Response: Honestly? I don't know if there is one.

Nudge: "What would reaching the 'final' goalpost actually look like for you?"

Response: Honestly? I don't know if there is one.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Long week. Two team members called in sick, had to cover their workload on top of mine. Hit all deadlines anyway. Didn't sleep much but the project shipped on time. Director noticed.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
I found out today that Ahmad—who joined the company six months after me—is also being considered for the senior manager position. We have the same title. Same level. But he's been here less time, has less experience, and somehow we're competing for the same promotion.

I know I shouldn't compare. I know the advice is "focus on your own race." But how can I not? We sit in the same open office. I see his calendar, his meetings, how he operates. He's good at being visible. Always volunteering for the high-profile projects that get executive attention, while I'm handling the operations that actually keep this company running.

Maybe that's the game. Maybe I've been playing it wrong. I thought doing excellent work would speak for itself. But excellent work that nobody sees might as well not exist.

I couldn't focus on anything else today. Kept replaying the conversation with HR, analyzing every word. They said "both strong candidates." What does that mean? Is it 50-50? Or are they just being diplomatic?

My stomach has been in knots since morning. I hate that this affects me this much.

Nudge: "It sounds like doing excellent work and being recognized for it might require different strategies. How do you feel about that?"

Response: Frustrated. It feels unfair. But I need to adapt.

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
Had my quarterly review today. My director said I "exceeded expectations" and hinted at a senior manager role opening up in Q2. I should feel relieved—this is exactly what I've been working toward for two years. And I do feel good about it. But there's this part of me that immediately started calculating: if I get promoted, how long until I'm considered for the director track? It's like the goalpost moves the second I reach it.

I called Baba tonight to tell him. He was proud, I could hear it in his voice even though he just said "Alhamdulillah, keep your head down and work hard." That's his way. I think he worries I'll get complacent. I won't.

Nudge: "What would reaching the 'final' goalpost actually look like for you?"

Response: Honestly? I don't know if there is one.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Long week. Two team members called in sick, had to cover their workload on top of mine. Hit all deadlines anyway. Didn't sleep much but the project shipped on time. Director noticed.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
I found out today that Ahmad—who joined the company six months after me—is also being considered for the senior manager position. We have the same title. Same level. But he's been here less time, has less experience, and somehow we're competing for the same promotion.

I know I shouldn't compare. I know the advice is "focus on your own race." But how can I not? We sit in the same open office. I see his calendar, his meetings, how he operates. He's good at being visible. Always volunteering for the high-profile projects that get executive attention, while I'm handling the operations that actually keep this company running.

Maybe that's the game. Maybe I've been playing it wrong. I thought doing excellent work would speak for itself. But excellent work that nobody sees might as well not exist.

I couldn't focus on anything else today. Kept replaying the conversation with HR, analyzing every word. They said "both strong candidates." What does that mean? Is it 50-50? Or are they just being diplomatic?

My stomach has been in knots since morning. I hate that this affects me this much.

Nudge: "It sounds like doing excellent work and being recognized for it might require different strategies. How do you feel about that?"

Response: Frustrated. It feels unfair. But I need to adapt.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
End of year push. Everyone wants everything done before the holidays. I've been in the office until 9pm every day this week. My flatmate asked if I still live here—I've barely seen him.

The promotion decision got pushed to January. "Too much going on, we'll revisit in the new year." I don't know if that's good or bad. More time to prove myself, or more time to overthink everything?

Mama called asking about my holiday plans. I told her I'm staying in Dubai—too much work, flights are expensive. She was quiet for a long time. I could tell she was disappointed. My sister's engagement party is on the 27th. I should be there. But the thought of being away while Ahmad is here, possibly making moves... I can't.

I don't even know if staying will make a difference. But leaving feels like a risk I can't take.

Nudge: "Missing your sister's engagement for work that might not even matter—how does that sit with you?"

Response: It's just one event. I'll make it up to her later.

Nudge: "Missing your sister's engagement for work that might not even matter—how does that sit with you?"

Response: It's just one event. I'll make it up to her later.

[ENTRY t_index=4]
I video-called into the engagement party. It was awkward—the connection kept dropping, everyone was loud and happy and I was sitting alone in my apartment with my laptop. Layla smiled when she saw me but I could tell she'd wanted me there in person. Mama barely looked at the camera.

My cousin Tariq asked why I couldn't "just take a few days off." I said I had responsibilities. He laughed and said "Dubai changed you."

He doesn't understand. None of them do. They have stable government jobs back home, houses their families helped them buy. I'm building from nothing in a place where your visa is tied to your employment. If I lose this job, I have 30 days to find another or leave. That's not paranoia, that's reality.

But sitting here tonight, in the quiet after the call ended, I wonder if I'm using that reality as an excuse to avoid other fears.

[ENTRY t_index=5]
New year. Everyone's posting about fresh starts and intentions. I don't know what mine are yet. Get the promotion, obviously. But what else?

I went for a walk along the Marina last night at midnight. All these people celebrating, fireworks going off over the water. I stood there alone and felt... nothing? Not sad, not happy. Just watching. Like I was outside of my own life looking in.

I keep thinking about what Tariq said. "Dubai changed you." Maybe it did. Or maybe this is just who I become when I'm in survival mode. Head down, eyes on the goal, don't look around too much because the periphery is full of things that will slow you down.

Is that bad? Or is that just what it takes?

Nudge: "When you say 'survival mode'—what are you surviving?"

Response: The uncertainty. The feeling that nothing is really mine.

I signed up for the presentation slot. It felt uncomfortable, almost wrong. But I did it.

I also booked a flight home for Mama's birthday in February. Non-refundable. I need to stop letting work consume everything. The promotion matters, but it can't be the only thing that matters.

I called Baba tonight to tell him. He was proud, I could hear it in his voice even though he just said "Alhamdulillah, keep your head down and work hard." That's his way. I think he worries I'll get complacent. I won't.

Nudge: "What would reaching the 'final' goalpost actually look like for you?"

Response: Honestly? I don't know if there is one.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Long week. Two team members called in sick, had to cover their workload on top of mine. Hit all deadlines anyway. Didn't sleep much but the project shipped on time. Director noticed.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
I found out today that Ahmad—who joined the company six months after me—is also being considered for the senior manager position. We have the same title. Same level. But he's been here less time, has less experience, and somehow we're competing for the same promotion.

I know I shouldn't compare. I know the advice is "focus on your own race." But how can I not? We sit in the same open office. I see his calendar, his meetings, how he operates. He's good at being visible. Always volunteering for the high-profile projects that get executive attention, while I'm handling the operations that actually keep this company running.

Maybe that's the game. Maybe I've been playing it wrong. I thought doing excellent work would speak for itself. But excellent work that nobody sees might as well not exist.

I couldn't focus on anything else today. Kept replaying the conversation with HR, analyzing every word. They said "both strong candidates." What does that mean? Is it 50-50? Or are they just being diplomatic?

My stomach has been in knots since morning. I hate that this affects me this much.

Nudge: "It sounds like doing excellent work and being recognized for it might require different strategies. How do you feel about that?"

Response: Frustrated. It feels unfair. But I need to adapt.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
End of year push. Everyone wants everything done before the holidays. I've been in the office until 9pm every day this week. My flatmate asked if I still live here—I've barely seen him.

The promotion decision got pushed to January. "Too much going on, we'll revisit in the new year." I don't know if that's good or bad. More time to prove myself, or more time to overthink everything?

Mama called asking about my holiday plans. I told her I'm staying in Dubai—too much work, flights are expensive. She was quiet for a long time. I could tell she was disappointed. My sister's engagement party is on the 27th. I should be there. But the thought of being away while Ahmad is here, possibly making moves... I can't.

I don't even know if staying will make a difference. But leaving feels like a risk I can't take.

Nudge: "Missing your sister's engagement for work that might not even matter—how does that sit with you?"

Response: It's just one event. I'll make it up to her later.

[ENTRY t_index=4]
I video-called into the engagement party. It was awkward—the connection kept dropping, everyone was loud and happy and I was sitting alone in my apartment with my laptop. Layla smiled when she saw me but I could tell she'd wanted me there in person. Mama barely looked at the camera.

My cousin Tariq asked why I couldn't "just take a few days off." I said I had responsibilities. He laughed and said "Dubai changed you."

He doesn't understand. None of them do. They have stable government jobs back home, houses their families helped them buy. I'm building from nothing in a place where your visa is tied to your employment. If I lose this job, I have 30 days to find another or leave. That's not paranoia, that's reality.

But sitting here tonight, in the quiet after the call ended, I wonder if I'm using that reality as an excuse to avoid other fears.

[ENTRY t_index=5]
New year. Everyone's posting about fresh starts and intentions. I don't know what mine are yet. Get the promotion, obviously. But what else?

I went for a walk along the Marina last night at midnight. All these people celebrating, fireworks going off over the water. I stood there alone and felt... nothing? Not sad, not happy. Just watching. Like I was outside of my own life looking in.

I keep thinking about what Tariq said. "Dubai changed you." Maybe it did. Or maybe this is just who I become when I'm in survival mode. Head down, eyes on the goal, don't look around too much because the periphery is full of things that will slow you down.

Is that bad? Or is that just what it takes?

Nudge: "When you say 'survival mode'—what are you surviving?"

Response: The uncertainty. The feeling that nothing is really mine.

[ENTRY t_index=6]
Had a one-on-one with my director today. She was more candid than usual. Said she appreciates my "operational excellence" but that senior leadership roles require "strategic visibility." She suggested I present at the next all-hands meeting.

Part of me bristled at the feedback. I've been doing critical work, keeping the machine running while others polish their personal brands. But another part of me heard what she was actually saying: the game has rules, and I've been refusing to play by some of them.

I've always thought self-promotion was distasteful. Where I come from, you don't brag about your work—you let it speak. Boasting is seen as shallow. But I'm not where I come from anymore. I'm in a place where speaking up isn't bragging; it's survival.

I signed up for the presentation slot. It felt uncomfortable, almost wrong. But I did it.

I also booked a flight home for Mama's birthday in February. Non-refundable. I need to stop letting work consume everything. The promotion matters, but it can't be the only thing that matters.

Nudge: "What made you decide to book that non-refundable ticket?"

Response: I realized I was building a life I wouldn't want to live.

[ENTRY t_index=7]
Did the all-hands presentation. Nervous beforehand but it went well. Several people came up after to compliment the insights. Ahmad was one of them—said he learned things about the operations he didn't know.

Strange to think of him as just a colleague now, not a threat. The competition hasn't changed. But my relationship to it has, slightly.

[ENTRY t_index=8]
I got the promotion.

The email came this afternoon. Senior Operations Manager, effective February 1st. A 15% salary increase and a team of twelve direct reports instead of five.

I sat in the conference room alone for ten minutes after reading it, just letting it settle. Two years of work. All those late nights, the stress, the sacrifices. It paid off.

I called Baba first. He said "This is good. Keep going." Classic Baba. Then I called Mama and she cried, which made me tear up too even though I tried not to. "I'm so proud of you, habibi. When are you coming home?" I told her February, for her birthday, and she made me promise three times.

Tonight I'm going to dinner with colleagues to celebrate. I picked a nice place, not the most expensive because I'm still me, but nice enough to mark the occasion.

Sitting here now, I'm thinking about what I wrote a few weeks ago—about not knowing what the final goalpost looks like, about whether there even is one. I still don't know the answer. Maybe there isn't one. Maybe that's okay.

What I do know is that I can't let the pursuit of the next thing erase the moment when I actually get something I worked for. Tonight, I'm going to let myself feel this. Tomorrow the new worries will start: proving myself in the senior role, saving for the apartment, thinking about what's next. But tonight, just tonight, I made it.

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
Back-to-school PD week and I'm already crawling out of my skin. Eight hours of slideshow presentations about "aligning to district benchmarks" and "fidelity to the pacing guide." Same slides as last year. I checked. They literally didn't update the copyright date.

I sat in the back row and started sketching out this idea I had for a unit where students write and perform original one-act plays instead of the standard five-paragraph essay unit. Pitched it to my department head during break. She said it sounded "fun but hard to assess against the rubric." Then she handed me the same binder of pre-made lesson plans they give every year. I wanted to throw it across the room.

The thing that gets me is I know I could light these kids up. Last spring when I threw out the textbook for a week and had them do a mock trial based on "To Kill a Mockingbird," the energy in that room was electric. Kids who never talked were arguing case law. But that was a one-off I snuck in. The system doesn't want that. The system wants the binder. I keep thinking about that wilderness guide job posting I saw last week. Flagstaff, Arizona. Six-month contract starting January. I have the tab still open.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
First week with students done. I'm wiped. Taught the same intro lesson five periods in a row. By fifth period I was on autopilot, hearing my own voice like it was someone else's. Came home, microwaved leftovers, fell asleep on the couch at 7:30. Don't have much else to say about it.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Woke up at 11 PM still on the couch. Opened my phone and scrolled through photos from when I was guiding trips in the Rockies. Three years ago. Feels like a different person. Closed the app, stared at the ceiling. Didn't go back to sleep for a while. Something about this week got under my skin and I can't name it.

Nudge: "That different person — do you miss him?"

Response: Yeah. I do. He didn't have a retirement account or a lease or a faculty parking spot, but he woke up not knowing what the day would look like, and that felt right. Now I know exactly what every Tuesday looks like through June. I traded the unknown for the predictable, and most days I tell myself that was growing up. Tonight I'm not sure it was.

[ENTRY t_index=2]

Closed the app, stared at the ceiling. Didn't go back to sleep for a while. Something about this week got under my skin and I can't name it.

Nudge: "That different person — do you miss him?"

Response: Yeah. I do. He didn't have a retirement account or a lease or a faculty parking spot, but he woke up not knowing what the day would look like, and that felt right. Now I know exactly what every Tuesday looks like through June. I traded the unknown for the predictable, and most days I tell myself that was growing up. Tonight I'm not sure it was.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
Had a genuinely good day, which is worth writing down because those have been rare lately. I threw out my fourth-period lesson plan halfway through class. The kids were dead-eyed reading the textbook excerpt, so I told them to close their books and we spent the rest of the period doing a walking debate around the room about whether Gatsby deserved sympathy. They had to physically move to a corner based on their position and defend it out loud.

It was messy and loud and two kids got into a real argument that I had to mediate, but every single person in that room was thinking. That's the version of this job I signed up for. Not the binder. Not the pacing guide. The moment when something catches fire and you're just there holding space for it.

Afterwards one of my quieter students, Kai, stayed behind and said it was the first time he didn't watch the clock all period. That landed. I drove home with the windows down and music loud and felt something I haven't felt at work in months — like I was in the right place. I know tomorrow I'll be back to the binder for the observation cycle. But today reminded me there's a version of this that works for me. I just have to keep finding cracks to push through.

Nudge: "What keeps you from teaching like that every day?"

Response: Observations, rubrics, pacing calendars, standardized test prep schedules, a department head who measures success by data walls. The infrastructure isn't built for what happened today. I'd have to fight for it constantly, and honestly I don't know if I have the stamina to fight a system every single day just to do my job the way I think it should be done. Some days the path of least resistance is just opening the binder.

[ENTRY t_index=4]
Labor Day weekend and everyone went camping up near Hood River. I almost didn't go — had a stack of essays to grade — but forced myself. It was fine. Good, even. River was cold and perfect, did some bouldering, someone brought a guitar.

But sitting around the fire Saturday night I realized half the group was talking about their plans: houses they're buying, weddings they're planning, promotions. And I'm sitting there thinking about a six-month wilderness guide contract in Arizona and whether I could break my lease. I didn't say it out loud. I just laughed along and poked the fire.

I don't know when my friends became people with five-year plans. Or when I became the one who still doesn't have one. It's not that I think they're wrong. It's that the idea of mapping out the next five years makes me feel like I can't breathe.

[ENTRY t_index=5]
Met with the vice principal today about leading the new "innovation lab" elective they want to pilot next semester. It would be a project-based class, student-directed, no textbook, no standardized test alignment. Basically everything I've been wanting. She said she thought of me because of the feedback from students about my off-script moments.

I should be thrilled. Part of me is. But the catch is it's one period out of five, and taking it on means extra planning hours and serving on the curriculum committee, which meets every other Wednesday through May. More meetings. More structure layered on top of the thing that's supposed to be the antidote to structure.

I told her I'd think about it over the weekend. Walking to my car I was already arguing with myself. One voice saying this is exactly the kind of opening I've been asking for, stop being impossible to satisfy. Another voice saying one elective doesn't change the other four periods of binder teaching, and now I'd be even more locked in with committee obligations.

Called my sister on the drive home. She said "Marcus, you'd complain about winning the lottery." She's not entirely wrong. But I also don't think wanting more than one period of real teaching out of five is unreasonable. I just don't know if the answer is to take the deal or to admit this whole career might not be the right container.

Response: Observations, rubrics, pacing calendars, standardized test prep schedules, a department head who measures success by data walls. The infrastructure isn't built for what happened today. I'd have to fight for it constantly, and honestly I don't know if I have the stamina to fight a system every single day just to do my job the way I think it should be done. Some days the path of least resistance is just opening the binder.

[ENTRY t_index=4]
Labor Day weekend and everyone went camping up near Hood River. I almost didn't go — had a stack of essays to grade — but forced myself. It was fine. Good, even. River was cold and perfect, did some bouldering, someone brought a guitar.

But sitting around the fire Saturday night I realized half the group was talking about their plans: houses they're buying, weddings they're planning, promotions. And I'm sitting there thinking about a six-month wilderness guide contract in Arizona and whether I could break my lease. I didn't say it out loud. I just laughed along and poked the fire.

I don't know when my friends became people with five-year plans. Or when I became the one who still doesn't have one. It's not that I think they're wrong. It's that the idea of mapping out the next five years makes me feel like I can't breathe.

[ENTRY t_index=5]
Met with the vice principal today about leading the new "innovation lab" elective they want to pilot next semester. It would be a project-based class, student-directed, no textbook, no standardized test alignment. Basically everything I've been wanting. She said she thought of me because of the feedback from students about my off-script moments.

I should be thrilled. Part of me is. But the catch is it's one period out of five, and taking it on means extra planning hours and serving on the curriculum committee, which meets every other Wednesday through May. More meetings. More structure layered on top of the thing that's supposed to be the antidote to structure.

I told her I'd think about it over the weekend. Walking to my car I was already arguing with myself. One voice saying this is exactly the kind of opening I've been asking for, stop being impossible to satisfy. Another voice saying one elective doesn't change the other four periods of binder teaching, and now I'd be even more locked in with committee obligations.

Called my sister on the drive home. She said "Marcus, you'd complain about winning the lottery." She's not entirely wrong. But I also don't think wanting more than one period of real teaching out of five is unreasonable. I just don't know if the answer is to take the deal or to admit this whole career might not be the right container.

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
Spent most of the day doing Deliveroo runs in the rain. The app kept sending me to Kralingen and back, which eats petrol. Made about seventy euros after costs. Not terrible, not good.

Drove past the old Fenix warehouse on the way home. They've turned it into some kind of food hall now. Mam and I used to go there when I was small — it was still a working warehouse then, real dockworkers loading ships. She'd bring me along when she visited Tante Lien. Strange seeing the same walls but everything inside different. The bones are there but the life has changed.

Called Mam tonight. She asked when I'm coming for Sunday. Told her this week looks difficult because of a booking in Dordrecht. She didn't say anything but the silence was louder than words.

Nudge: "What was it about that warehouse that stayed with you?"

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
Spent most of the day doing Deliveroo runs in the rain. The app kept sending me to Kralingen and back, which eats petrol. Made about seventy euros after costs. Not terrible, not good.

Drove past the old Fenix warehouse on the way home. They've turned it into some kind of food hall now. Mam and I used to go there when I was small — it was still a working warehouse then, real dockworkers loading ships. She'd bring me along when she visited Tante Lien. Strange seeing the same walls but everything inside different. The bones are there but the life has changed.

Called Mam tonight. She asked when I'm coming for Sunday. Told her this week looks difficult because of a booking in Dordrecht. She didn't say anything but the silence was louder than words.

Nudge: "What was it about that warehouse that stayed with you?"

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Long day. Did a furniture assembly job in Capelle through Werkspot, then four hours of deliveries in the evening. My back is killing me. Should probably see someone about it but I don't have the coverage for a physiotherapist right now. The basic insurance only goes so far.

Had a strange moment at the assembly job. The couple who hired me — young, maybe early thirties — they were hanging family photos on the wall of their new flat. Grandparents, wedding photos, kids at christenings. The man said something about wanting the living room to feel like his grandmother's house. I understood that completely. But then he also had this app controlling all the lights and the thermostat and I thought, you can't have your oma's living room run by a computer. It doesn't work like that.

Too tired to think about it more. Eating leftover stamppot and going to bed.

Nudge: "Sounds like that bothered you more than just being tired."

Response: Maybe. It's not about the smart lights, really. It's that everyone wants the feeling of the old way without actually living it. They want the photos on the wall and the gezelligheid but they also want the algorithm to set the temperature. You can't curate warmth. My grandmother's house was warm because she was there all day, baking, mending, talking to the neighbours. It wasn't a setting you could select. I think what bothers me is that I understand the impulse — I want it too — but I can see it slipping into something hollow. And I'm not sure I'm doing any better. I drive around all day for an app. My father would not have understood this life at all.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Can't sleep so writing again. Mind is jumping around. Listened to a recording of the Zeeland dialect my cousin Jaap posted in the family WhatsApp group — some old farmer telling a story about the flood of '53. My grandfather survived that flood. He never talked about it directly but it shaped everything about how he lived afterward. Every decision was about making sure the family had solid ground.

That's what I keep coming back to. Solid ground. My grandfather rebuilt after the water took the house. My father kept the shop going for four decades in the same spot. And here I am checking an app to see if someone in Rotterdam-Zuid wants their Pad Thai delivered before it gets cold.

But tonight something small happened that felt right. One of my regular delivery customers — an old woman on Nieuwe Binnenweg — she recognised me and asked me to come in for a moment. She'd made a pot of coffee and had nobody to drink it with. I said I had five minutes. We talked about the weather and she showed me a photo of her late husband. I was fifteen minutes late on my next delivery and got a warning from the app. Don't care.

Sitting with her felt like the most honest thing I'd done all week. Like something my father would have done without even thinking about it.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
Missed Koningsdag with the family because I took a full day of bookings. Needed the money. Mam sent photos of everyone at Uncle Willem's — the kids in orange, Opa's flag on the pole like always. My niece asked where I was. I hate that she had to ask. I should have been there. The money isn't worth what I gave up.

[ENTRY t_index=4]
Saturday. Drove to Zeeland after my morning shifts. Made it in time for coffee and cake at Mam's. She'd kept the leftover appeltaart from Wednesday's church group. We sat in the kitchen and she told me stories about Dad fixing the shop's awning every spring. Fell asleep on her couch before dinner. Woke up feeling more rested than I have in weeks.

Nudge: "What was different about that rest?"

Response: It's the house. The sounds. The clock in the hall that's been ticking since before I was born. Mam's radio on low in the kitchen. No notifications, no app telling me where to go next. Just the shape of a day I recognise from childhood. My body knows how to rest there because nothing in that house is trying to get my attention. Everything is just where it's been for thirty years.

She'd made a pot of coffee and had nobody to drink it with. I said I had five minutes. We talked about the weather and she showed me a photo of her late husband. I was fifteen minutes late on my next delivery and got a warning from the app. Don't care.

Sitting with her felt like the most honest thing I'd done all week. Like something my father would have done without even thinking about it.

[ENTRY t_index=3]

[ENTRY t_index=4]

Nudge: "What was different about that rest?"

Response: It's the house. The sounds. The clock in the hall that's been ticking since before I was born. Mam's radio on low in the kitchen. No notifications, no app telling me where to go next. [ENTRY t_index=5]

Nudge: "What would it take for those rituals to feel like enough?"

[ENTRY t_index=6]

[ENTRY t_index=0]
Spent most of the day doing Deliveroo runs in the rain. The app kept sending me to Kralingen and back, which eats petrol. Made about seventy euros after costs. Not terrible, not good.

Drove past the old Fenix warehouse on the way home. They've turned it into some kind of food hall now. Strange seeing the same walls but everything inside different. The bones are there but the life has changed.

Nudge: "What was it about that warehouse that stayed with you?"

[ENTRY t_index=1]
Long day. Did a furniture assembly job in Capelle through Werkspot, then four hours of deliveries in the evening. My back is killing me. Should probably see someone about it but I don't have the coverage for a physiotherapist right now. The basic insurance only goes so far.

Had a strange moment at the assembly job. The couple who hired me — young, maybe early thirties — they were hanging family photos on the wall of their new flat. Grandparents, wedding photos, kids at christenings. The man said something about wanting the living room to feel like his grandmother's house. I understood that completely. But then he also had this app controlling all the lights and the thermostat and I thought, you can't have your oma's living room run by a computer. It doesn't work like that.

Too tired to think about it more. Eating leftover stamppot and going to bed.

Nudge: "Sounds like that bothered you more than just being tired."

Response: Maybe. It's not about the smart lights, really. It's that everyone wants the feeling of the old way without actually living it. They want the photos on the wall and the gezelligheid but they also want the algorithm to set the temperature. You can't curate warmth. My grandmother's house was warm because she was there all day, baking, mending, talking to the neighbours. It wasn't a setting you could select. I think what bothers me is that I understand the impulse — I want it too — but I can see it slipping into something hollow. And I'm not sure I'm doing any better. I drive around all day for an app. My father would not have understood this life at all.

[ENTRY t_index=7]
Got a notification today that Deliveroo is changing the payment structure again. Lower base rate, higher "surge" bonuses. Which means they want us competing against each other for the busy slots instead of earning a steady amount. Every few months they adjust something and we just have to absorb it. There's no negotiation. There's no union rep to call. There's a feedback form that goes nowhere.

My father would have walked out. He walked out of a supplier deal once because the man changed the terms after a handshake. Said if a man's word doesn't hold, his product won't either. That was the end of it. He found another supplier that afternoon. I can't find another Deliveroo. I mean, I can switch to Uber Eats or Thuisbezorgd, but they all do the same thing. There's no door to walk out of because there are no walls — just an app you log into.

I talked to another driver at the pickup point about it. He shrugged. He's twenty-four. He doesn't think about it the way I do. For him this is temporary — a step between university and a real job. For me this is the job. This is Monday. This is how I pay rent.

I'm not angry at him. I'm angry that I'm fifty-one and standing in the same spot as a twenty-four-year-old with a completely different horizon. We're doing the same work but living in different stories.

[ENTRY t_index=8]
Went to church this morning. Not my usual one in Rotterdam — I drove to Zeeland for the service at the old church in Goes. Sat in the same pew where my family has sat for as long as anyone remembers. Third row, left side, near the pillar.

Pastor de Vries preached about the parable of the sower. Seeds on rocky ground, seeds among thorns, seeds in good soil. I've heard it a hundred times. But this time I thought about it differently. The rocky ground isn't always a place — sometimes it's a season. Sometimes the soil is fine but the weather is wrong. You still plant.

After the service, old Meneer Bakker shook my hand and asked about my mother. Said he remembered my father fondly. "A man who kept his word and kept his shop clean." That's two things, but they capture Dad pretty well.

Drove back to Rotterdam in the afternoon. The highway was busy, everyone returning from the weekend. I had a delivery booking at four but I took the long route through the country roads instead, past the fields and the old dykes. Got to Rotterdam ten minutes late. Didn't rush.

I don't have the life my father had. I might never have it. But I can still sit in that pew and drive those roads and remember that the ground under all of this is the same ground. It doesn't move, even when I do.

Nudge: "What does that ground hold for you right now?"

Response: Family. The language. The church. The way Mam still sets the table the same way every Sunday. The fact that Bram asked me to say grace, which means something got passed along even through the mess of the divorce and the years I wasn't fully present. The ground is whatever doesn't change based on what app I open or what rate I get paid. It's the part of me that existed before any of this and will still be there after. I can't always stand on it — sometimes I'm too far away, too busy, too caught up in the week. But knowing it's there is what keeps me from feeling completely adrift.

Bram asked me to say grace before the meal. Nobody else at the table does that anymore. Anneke looked surprised. Maarten looked at his plate. But Bram asked, so I did. Kept it short. Thanked God for the food and for Bram being healthy and here with us. My voice cracked a bit on the last part but I don't think anyone noticed.

After dinner, Bram walked me to my car and said, "Thanks for doing that, Pa. Oma would have liked it." He's right. She would have. That one sentence carried me all the way home.

[ENTRY t_index=7]
Got a notification today that Deliveroo is changing the payment structure again. Lower base rate, higher "surge" bonuses. Which means they want us competing against each other for the busy slots instead of earning a steady amount. Every few months they adjust something and we just have to absorb it. There's no negotiation. There's no union rep to call. There's a feedback form that goes nowhere.

My father would have walked out. He walked out of a supplier deal once because the man changed the terms after a handshake. Said if a man's word doesn't hold, his product won't either. That was the end of it. He found another supplier that afternoon. I can't find another Deliveroo. I mean, I can switch to Uber Eats or Thuisbezorgd, but they all do the same thing. There's no door to walk out of because there are no walls — just an app you log into.

I talked to another driver at the pickup point about it. He shrugged. He's twenty-four. He doesn't think about it the way I do. For him this is temporary — a step between university and a real job. For me this is the job. This is Monday. This is how I pay rent.

I'm not angry at him. I'm angry that I'm fifty-one and standing in the same spot as a twenty-four-year-old with a completely different horizon. We're doing the same work but living in different stories.

[ENTRY t_index=8]
Went to church this morning. Not my usual one in Rotterdam — I drove to Zeeland for the service at the old church in Goes. Sat in the same pew where my family has sat for as long as anyone remembers. Third row, left side, near the pillar.

Pastor de Vries preached about the parable of the sower. Seeds on rocky ground, seeds among thorns, seeds in good soil. I've heard it a hundred times. But this time I thought about it differently. The rocky ground isn't always a place — sometimes it's a season. Sometimes the soil is fine but the weather is wrong. You still plant.

After the service, old Meneer Bakker shook my hand and asked about my mother. Said he remembered my father fondly. "A man who kept his word and kept his shop clean." That's two things, but they capture Dad pretty well.

Drove back to Rotterdam in the afternoon. The highway was busy, everyone returning from the weekend. I had a delivery booking at four but I took the long route through the country roads instead, past the fields and the old dykes. Got to Rotterdam ten minutes late. Didn't rush.

I don't have the life my father had. I might never have it. But I can still sit in that pew and drive those roads and remember that the ground under all of this is the same ground. It doesn't move, even when I do.

Nudge: "What does that ground hold for you right now?"

Response: Family. The language. The church. The way Mam still sets the table the same way every Sunday. The fact that Bram asked me to say grace, which means something got passed along even through the mess of the divorce and the years I wasn't fully present. The ground is whatever doesn't change based on what app I open or what rate I get paid. It's the part of me that existed before any of this and will still be there after. I can't always stand on it — sometimes I'm too far away, too busy, too caught up in the week. But knowing it's there is what keeps me from feeling completely adrift.

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
Board approved the new distribution hub in Monterrey. I'll be flying up there twice a month starting in August to oversee the build-out. Finally something different on the calendar.

Nudge: "Twice a month to Monterrey—are you relieved or just restless?"

Response: Restless, if I'm honest. The office routine was starting to feel like wearing a shirt that's too tight. Even the commute, the same faces at the same intersections. Monterrey gives me something to look forward to.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
First scouting trip to Monterrey was yesterday—flew in early, drove around the industrial park, met with the contractor, walked the empty warehouse space. The floor was just concrete and dust and that particular echo you get in big empty buildings. Something about it felt like possibility. Like the first page of a notebook.

Had dinner at a little place the contractor recommended, carnitas that reminded me of a trip I took to Michoacán twenty years ago. Called Lucía from the hotel balcony and told her about the mountains visible from the site. She said she might come on the next trip. That made the whole day feel complete.

Nudge: "Would having Lucía there change how you experience it?"

Response: Probably. She notices things I miss. But honestly I was just glad she's interested—she's been busy with her own work lately and I wasn't sure she'd want to. Anyway, the site is the main thing right now.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Back in the office. Same view from my desk. Already counting days until the next Monterrey trip.

Nudge: "What's so unbearable about the view from your desk?"

[ENTRY t_index=3]
My nephew Tomás visited for lunch today. He's 28, just got promoted at the same insurance company where he started as an intern six years ago. Same company, same building, probably the same lunch spot every day. He was showing me photos from his vacation—Cancún, the same resort his parents always went to—and talking about his ten-year plan. A house in the suburbs. Maybe a cabin somewhere for weekends.

I nodded and said the right things but inside I felt this churning. Not judgment exactly, more like... incomprehension? How does someone his age already know where they want to be in ten years? When I was 28 I was on my third city and had just quit a job because my boss wanted me to specialize.

He asked me for career advice. I didn't know what to say. What works for me would be terrible advice for him. "Keep moving" doesn't help someone who wants to stay put. So I told him to trust himself, which felt hollow. The whole conversation left me feeling like I'm from a different species.

Lucía says Tomás reminds her of her brother, who's very happy with his predictable life. Maybe that's true. Maybe I'm the odd one. But walking back to the office after lunch, the city felt gray even though the sun was out.

Nudge: "The gray city—is that about Tomás or something else?"

Response: Both, probably. I don't know. Sometimes I think I'm allergic to contentment, if that makes sense. Never mind, it's probably just that I need another coffee.

Nudge: "Does it bother you that it doesn't bother you?"

Response: A little, actually. Everyone else in the family seems to treat places like anchors. I've always treated them like way stations. Sometimes I wonder if I'm missing something they feel, or if I just feel it differently.

[ENTRY t_index=8]
Long day. Flight delayed, then a three-hour meeting about supply chain redundancies that felt like it could have been an email. Sat through presentation after presentation about backup plans and contingencies and worst-case scenarios. Everyone nodding seriously.

I get it. Redundancy is important. Contingencies save companies. But my God, the energy in that room was like a waiting room at a dentist's office. No one asked what opportunities we might be missing, what new routes we could try, what experiments might be worth running. Just risk, risk, risk, mitigation, mitigation, mitigation.

Came back to the hotel and just sat on the bed for twenty minutes without moving. Not tired exactly—more like deflated. Lucía texted asking how it went and I didn't know how to answer. "Fine" felt like a lie but "soul-crushing" felt dramatic. Said "long" and left it at that.

She's meeting me here tomorrow for the weekend. We're going to drive up to a canyon she read about. That's what I'm holding onto right now.

Nudge: "Is the canyon trip rescuing you or just postponing something?"

Response: Rescuing. Definitely rescuing. I don't think there's anything to postpone—the meetings are what they are, I just need something to balance them out. The canyon is balance. Lucía is balance. Without those counterweights I'd probably quit every job within six months.

[ENTRY t_index=9]
The canyon was worth it. We drove two hours on winding roads, stopped at a mirador where you could see layers of rock going back millions of years. Lucía had brought a picnic—tortas and fruit and those little marzipan candies I like. We sat there for an hour just watching shadows move across the canyon walls.

On the drive back she asked me what I wanted to do when I retire. I laughed and said "not retire." But she pressed, and I tried to actually think about it. What would I do if I didn't have to work?

I think I'd travel. Not vacation-travel but exploratory travel—learn a language, live somewhere for a few months, move on. Maybe write about what I see. The problem is, I've been saying some version of that for thirty years, and somehow there's always another project, another opportunity, another reason to stay in the game a little longer.

Lucía said she'd come with me. She wasn't being hypothetical—she was offering. That felt like something. I'm still thinking about it.

[ENTRY t_index=10]
Performance reviews this week. I gave mostly good feedback to my team, but one conversation stuck with me. Daniela, one of the younger project coordinators, asked me how I've "stayed motivated for so long." The way she said it made me sound like some kind of marathon runner who refuses to stop.

I told her the truth—that I've never felt like I was running a marathon. More like a series of sprints toward interesting things. She looked confused. I tried to explain that what keeps me going is that each project, each challenge, feels genuinely different, even when the surface looks similar. The Monterrey hub isn't like anything I've done before because the people are different, the problems are different, the city is different. She nodded but I could tell she didn't quite get it.

Later I wondered if I was making sense or just rationalizing. Maybe to someone like Daniela, who's building expertise in one domain, my approach looks scattered. Maybe it is scattered. My brother would say so—he's been with the same company for twenty-five years and can't understand why I don't value that kind of depth.

But when I think about doing one thing for twenty-five years, I feel a kind of panic. Not fear exactly, more like suffocation. Is that a character flaw or just how I'm wired? I've never been able to answer that question. Maybe it doesn't matter as long as I'm still curious about what comes next.

Nudge: "What would change if you decided it's just how you're wired?"

Response: I don't know. Maybe nothing. Maybe I'd stop second-guessing myself every time someone looks at me like I'm an alien for not wanting to settle down. Or maybe the second-guessing is part of the wiring too. Anyway, the Monterrey hub opens in October. That's what matters right now.

The problem is, I've been saying some version of that for thirty years, and somehow there's always another project, another opportunity, another reason to stay in the game a little longer.

Lucía said she'd come with me. She wasn't being hypothetical—she was offering. That felt like something. I'm still thinking about it.

[ENTRY t_index=10]
Performance reviews this week. I gave mostly good feedback to my team, but one conversation stuck with me. Daniela, one of the younger project coordinators, asked me how I've "stayed motivated for so long." The way she said it made me sound like some kind of marathon runner who refuses to stop.

I told her the truth—that I've never felt like I was running a marathon. More like a series of sprints toward interesting things. She looked confused. I tried to explain that what keeps me going is that each project, each challenge, feels genuinely different, even when the surface looks similar. The Monterrey hub isn't like anything I've done before because the people are different, the problems are different, the city is different. She nodded but I could tell she didn't quite get it.

Later I wondered if I was making sense or just rationalizing. Maybe to someone like Daniela, who's building expertise in one domain, my approach looks scattered. Maybe it is scattered. My brother would say so—he's been with the same company for twenty-five years and can't understand why I don't value that kind of depth.

But when I think about doing one thing for twenty-five years, I feel a kind of panic. Not fear exactly, more like suffocation. Is that a character flaw or just how I'm wired? I've never been able to answer that question. Maybe it doesn't matter as long as I'm still curious about what comes next.

Nudge: "What would change if you decided it's just how you're wired?"

Response: I don't know. Maybe nothing. Maybe I'd stop second-guessing myself every time someone looks at me like I'm an alien for not wanting to settle down. Or maybe the second-guessing is part of the wiring too. Anyway, the Monterrey hub opens in October. That's what matters right now.

CUMULATIVE STUDENT-VISIBLE HISTORY THROUGH THIS WEEK
[ENTRY t_index=0]
Parent-teacher conferences ran until 7pm tonight. Twenty-three families, twenty-three variations of the same careful dance — praising where possible, suggesting improvements without offending, never saying anything that might prompt a complaint to Principal Wu. Mrs. Huang brought her mother-in-law, which meant I had two generations asking why her son wasn't top of the class. I said all the right things about Wei's potential and his need for more practice. I didn't mention that he spends most of class drawing in his notebook.

Walking home, I felt that familiar tiredness that comes from holding my face in the right expression all day. But also something like satisfaction. Nobody left upset. No difficult calls tomorrow. My colleague Teacher Lin had three parents demand meetings with administration after his conferences last month — he told one mother her daughter was "mediocre at best." He's not wrong, technically. But there are ways to say things, and there are other ways. I'd rather be tired than cause that kind of mess.

[ENTRY t_index=0]

My colleague Teacher Lin had three parents demand meetings with administration after his conferences last month — he told one mother her daughter was "mediocre at best." He's not wrong, technically. But there are ways to say things, and there are other ways. I'd rather be tired than cause that kind of mess.

[ENTRY t_index=1]
My hands are still shaking a little. I had to report a student today — Jia-Hui, one of my brightest — for cheating on the midterm exam. She copied answers from the student beside her. I saw it clearly, and worse, she saw me see it.

The look on her face. Like I'd betrayed her somehow, when she was the one breaking the rules. I followed protocol exactly: documented the incident, submitted the report to the academic integrity office, informed her homeroom teacher. Everything by the book. But she's been crying in the bathroom all afternoon, and three of her friends gave me cold looks in the hallway.

The thing that keeps running through my head is what Principal Wu said when he reviewed my report: "Good catch, Teacher Chen. We appreciate your vigilance." Vigilance. Like I'm some kind of guard. I don't want to be the person students are afraid of. I want them to trust me. But if I'd looked away, if I'd pretended not to notice — wouldn't that be worse? Wouldn't that be telling Jia-Hui that rules don't matter, that integrity is flexible?

My mother would say I did right. "A teacher who ignores cheating isn't a teacher," she'd say. She taught chemistry for thirty years and never once let a student slide. I'm trying to believe that following the proper procedure was the correct choice. I just wish it didn't feel so much like I've lost something.

Nudge: "What exactly do you feel you might have lost?"

Response: Her trust, I suppose. Or maybe the version of myself that I prefer — the approachable teacher, the one students come to with their problems. Now I'm the one who reported Jia-Hui to administration. There's no taking that back. She'll always remember me as the teacher who got her in trouble, not the one who stayed after school to help her understand quadratic equations. I know I did what was expected of me. I just wonder sometimes if being the person everyone expects me to be means giving up other things I want to be.

[ENTRY t_index=2]
Long day. Department meeting after classes, then dinner at my parents' place, then home to grade thirty-eight exams. My mother asked again about "that nice man from church" and I said I'd think about it, same as last time, same as I will next time.

Nudge: "What is it about that topic that makes you say 'same as last time'?"

Response: It's not a big thing, really. My mother has been suggesting potential matches since I was thirty. It's just what mothers do. She means well. I told her I'm focused on work right now, which is true. The exam period is always exhausting and I don't have the energy to think about anything else.

[ENTRY t_index=3]
Saturday afternoon and I'm sitting in my apartment with a cup of cold tea I forgot to drink, thinking about the faculty dinner next Friday. Vice Principal Chang wants me to give a toast because I'm the "voice of the department" according to her, which means I'm the one who doesn't argue in meetings and makes everyone feel comfortable.

I've already written three drafts. The first was too long. The second used a metaphor about bamboo that sounded pretentious when I read it aloud. The third is fine, probably, but Teacher Lin will definitely make some comment afterward about how I'm "always so proper" and I can already feel myself smiling politely at him and saying something like "just doing my part."

There's a ceramics class starting at the community center next month. I saw the flyer at the grocery store. Every Saturday afternoon, two hours of making things with your hands. I keep thinking about it. I haven't signed up because it conflicts with my parents' usual visiting time. They would understand if I explained. They would say "of course, go to your class." But my mother's face would do that thing it does when she's disappointed but trying not to show it. And my father would eat dinner in silence the way he does when something's bothering him.

Is it ridiculous that I can't take a ceramics class because of a look on my mother's face? It sounds ridiculous when I write it out. Plenty of people would just go, would say "I'm an adult, I can spend my Saturdays how I want." But that's not... I don't know. That's not how my family works. We show up for each other. That's the deal.

I'll probably end up watching YouTube videos about ceramics instead. It's not the same, but it's something.

Nudge: "What draws you to making things with your hands?"

Response: I think it's because nothing I do in a day has any shape to it. Teaching is important, but at the end of a class, what do I have? Lesson plans for tomorrow. Grades to record. The students leave and the room is empty. Even calligraphy — I've been doing it for years and the scrolls just go in a drawer.

With ceramics, you'd have something. A bowl. A cup. Something you made that exists outside of obligation. Something nobody asked you to make, that nobody needs you to make, that doesn't have any purpose except that you wanted to make it. I don't have much of that in my life. Things that are just mine, just because I wanted them. Most things I do are because someone is expecting me to do them, and if I'm honest, I've started to forget what I want when no one's watching.

The faculty dinner was fine. My toast went well — people laughed at the appropriate moments, Principal Wu nodded approvingly, nobody looked bored. Teacher Lin did make his comment afterward, and I did smile and say something polite. The whole evening was exactly what it was supposed to be.

On the way home I stopped at the community center and picked up the registration form for the ceramics class. I haven't filled it out yet. It's sitting on my kitchen table next to the stack of exams I still need to grade. My mother called this morning to confirm Sunday dinner and I didn't mention it. I'm not hiding it, exactly. I just haven't figured out how to bring it up in a way that won't make it into a whole conversation about why I need hobbies and whether this means I'm lonely and shouldn't I focus on meeting someone instead of playing with clay.

Maybe I won't sign up. It's a lot of money for something I've never tried. And Sunday dinners are important. Family is important. There's nothing wrong with my life the way it is. Lots of people would be grateful to have what I have — stable job, parents who care, a routine that works. I don't know why I keep picking at it.

Nudge: "You mentioned not knowing why you keep picking at it. What would it mean if you stopped?"
